functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Tm
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval of a chemical's melting
point. Lookup is based on CASRNs. Will automatically select a data
source to use if no method is provided; returns None if the data is not
available. Function has data for approximately 83000 chemicals.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

Tm

: Melting temperature, [K]

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined by the vairable
  `Tm_all_methods`.

# Notes

The available sources are as follows:

> - 'OPEN_NTBKM, a compillation of data on organics
>   as published in [^footnote-1] as Open Notebook Melting Points; Averaged
>   (median) values were used when
>   multiple points were available. For more information on this
>   invaluable and excellent collection, see
>   <http://onswebservices.wikispaces.com/meltingpoint>.
> - 'CRC_ORG', a compillation of data on organics
>   as published in [^footnote-2].
> - 'CRC_INORG', a compillation of data on
>   inorganic as published in [^footnote-2].
> - 'WEBBOOK', a NIST resource [^footnote-6] containing mostly experimental
>   and averaged values
> - 'WIKIDATA', data from the Wikidata project [^footnote-3]
> - 'COMMON_CHEMISTRY', a project from the CAS [^footnote-4]
> - 'JOBACK', an estimation method for organic substances in [^footnote-5]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Tm(CASRN='7732-18-5')
273.15
```

# See Also

Tm_methods

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bradley, Jean-Claude, Antony Williams, and Andrew Lang.
    "Jean-Claude Bradley Open Melting Point Dataset", May 20, 2014.
    <https://figshare.com/articles/Jean_Claude_Bradley_Open_Melting_Point_Datset/1031637>.

[^footnote-2]: Haynes, W.M., Thomas J. Bruno, and David R. Lide. CRC Handbook of
    Chemistry and Physics, 95E. Boca Raton, FL: CRC press, 2014.

[^footnote-3]: Wikidata. Wikidata. Accessed via API. <https://www.wikidata.org/>

[^footnote-4]: "CAS Common Chemistry". <https://commonchemistry.cas.org/>.

[^footnote-5]: Joback, K.G., and R.C. Reid. "Estimation of Pure-Component
    Properties from Group-Contributions." Chemical Engineering
    Communications 57, no. 1-6 (July 1, 1987): 233-43.
    doi:10.1080/00986448708960487.

[^footnote-6]: Shen, V.K., Siderius, D.W., Krekelberg, W.P., and Hatch, H.W., Eds.,
    NIST WebBook, NIST, <http://doi.org/10.18434/T4M88Q>

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Clapeyron
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at arbitrary temperatures using the
Clapeyron equation.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta H_{vap} = RT \Delta Z \frac{\ln (P_c/Psat)}{(1-T_{r})}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

dZ

: Change in compressibility factor between liquid and gas, []

Psat

: Saturation pressure of fluid [Pa], optional

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

No original source is available for this equation.
[^footnote-1] claims this equation overpredicts enthalpy by several percent.
Under Tr = 0.8, dZ = 1 is a reasonable assumption.
This equation is most accurate at the normal boiling point.

Internal units are bar.

WARNING: I believe it possible that the adjustment for pressure may be incorrect

# Examples

Problem from Perry's examples.

```pycon
>>> Clapeyron(T=294.0, Tc=466.0, Pc=5.55E6)
26512.36357131963
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.
## Enthalpy of Vaporization at T

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Pitzer
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at arbitrary temperatures using a
fit by [^footnote-2] to the work of Pitzer [^footnote-1]; requires a chemical's critical
temperature and acentric factor.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\frac{\Delta_{vap} H}{RT_c}=7.08(1-T_r)^{0.354}+10.95\omega(1-T_r)^{0.456}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

omega

: Acentric factor [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

This equation is listed in [^footnote-3], page 2-487 as method #2 for estimating
Hvap. This cites [^footnote-2].

The recommended range is 0.6 to 1 Tr. Users should expect up to 5% error.
This function converges to zero at `Tc`. If Tc is larger than T,
0 is returned as the model would return complex numbers.

The original article has been reviewed and found to have a set of tabulated
values which could be used instead of the fit function to provide additional
accuracy.

# Examples

Example as in [^footnote-3], p2-487; exp: 37.51 kJ/mol

```pycon
>>> Pitzer(452, 645.6, 0.35017)
36696.749078320056
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Pitzer, Kenneth S. "The Volumetric and Thermodynamic Properties of
    Fluids. I. Theoretical Basis and Virial Coefficients."
    Journal of the American Chemical Society 77, no. 13 (July 1, 1955):
    3427-33. doi:10.1021/ja01618a001

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-3]: Green, Don, and Robert Perry. Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook,
    Eighth Edition. McGraw-Hill Professional, 2007.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.SMK
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at arbitrary temperatures using a
the work of [^footnote-1]; requires a chemical's critical temperature and
acentric factor.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} = \left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R1)} + \left( \frac{\omega - \omega^{(R1)}} {\omega^{(R2)} - \omega^{(R1)}} \right) \left[\left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R2)} - \left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R1)} \right]
$$

$$
\left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R1)} = 6.537 \tau^{1/3} - 2.467 \tau^{5/6} - 77.251 \tau^{1.208} + 59.634 \tau + 36.009 \tau^2 - 14.606 \tau^3
$$

$$
\left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R2)} - \left( \frac{\Delta H_{vap}} {RT_c} \right)^{(R1)}=-0.133 \tau^{1/3} - 28.215 \tau^{5/6} - 82.958 \tau^{1.208} + 99.00 \tau  + 19.105 \tau^2 -2.796 \tau^3
$$

$$
\tau = 1-T/T_c
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

omega

: Acentric factor [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

The original article has been reviewed and found to have coefficients with
slightly more precision. Additionally, the form of the equation is slightly
different, but numerically equivalent.

The refence fluids are:

$\omega_0$ = benzene = 0.212

$\omega_1$ = carbazole = 0.461

A sample problem in the article has been verified. The numerical result
presented by the author requires high numerical accuracy to obtain.

This function converges to zero at `Tc`. If Tc is larger than T,
0 is returned as the model would return complex numbers.

# Examples

Problem in [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> SMK(553.15, 751.35, 0.302)
39866.18999046229
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Sivaraman, Alwarappa, Joe W. Magee, and Riki Kobayashi. "Generalized
    Correlation of Latent Heats of Vaporization of Coal-Liquid Model Compounds
    between Their Freezing Points and Critical Points." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 23, no. 1 (February 1, 1984): 97-100.
    doi:10.1021/i100013a017.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.MK
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at arbitrary temperatures using a
the work of [^footnote-1]; requires a chemical's critical temperature and
acentric factor.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta H_{vap} =  \Delta H_{vap}^{(0)} + \omega \Delta H_{vap}^{(1)} + \omega^2 \Delta H_{vap}^{(2)}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta H_{vap}^{(i)}}{RT_c} = b^{(j)} \tau^{1/3} + b_2^{(j)} \tau^{5/6} + b_3^{(j)} \tau^{1.2083} + b_4^{(j)}\tau + b_5^{(j)} \tau^2 + b_6^{(j)} \tau^3
$$

$$
\tau = 1-T/T_c
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

omega

: Acentric factor [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

The original article has been reviewed. A total of 18 coefficients are used:

WARNING: The correlation has been implemented as described in the article,
but its results seem different and with some error.
Its results match with other functions however.

Has poor behavior for low-temperature use.
This function converges to zero at `Tc`. If Tc is larger than T,
0 is returned as the model would return complex numbers.

# Examples

Problem in article for SMK function.

```pycon
>>> MK(553.15, 751.35, 0.302)
38728.00667307733
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Morgan, David L., and Riki Kobayashi. "Extension of Pitzer CSP
    Models for Vapor Pressures and Heats of Vaporization to Long-Chain
    Hydrocarbons." Fluid Phase Equilibria 94 (March 15, 1994): 51-87.
    doi:10.1016/0378-3812(94)87051-9.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Velasco
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at arbitrary temperatures using a
the work of [^footnote-1]; requires a chemical's critical temperature and
acentric factor.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta_{vap} H = RT_c(7.2729 + 10.4962\omega + 0.6061\omega^2)(1-T_r)^{0.38}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

omega

: Acentric factor [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

The original article has been reviewed. It is regressed from enthalpy of
vaporization values at 0.7Tr, from 121 fluids in REFPROP 9.1.
A value in the article was read to be similar, but slightly too low from
that calculated here.
This function converges to zero at `Tc`. If Tc is larger than T,
0 is returned as the model would return complex numbers.

# Examples

From graph, in [^footnote-1] for perfluoro-n-heptane.

```pycon
>>> Velasco(333.2, 476.0, 0.5559)
33299.428636069264
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Velasco, S., M. J. Santos, and J. A. White. "Extended Corresponding
    States Expressions for the Changes in Enthalpy, Compressibility Factor
    and Constant-Volume Heat Capacity at Vaporization." The Journal of
    Chemical Thermodynamics 85 (June 2015): 68-76.
    doi:10.1016/j.jct.2015.01.011.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Riedel
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at the boiling point, using the
Ridel [^footnote-1] CSP method. Required information are critical temperature
and pressure, and boiling point. Equation taken from [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3].

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta_{vap} H=1.093 T_b R\frac{\ln P_c-1.013}{0.930-T_{br}}
$$

# Parameters

Tb

: Boiling temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point, [J/mol]

# Notes

This equation has no example calculation in any source. The source has not
been verified. It is equation 4-144 in Perry's. Perry's also claims that
errors seldom surpass 5%.

[^footnote-2] is the source of example work here, showing a calculation at 0.0%
error.

Internal units of pressure are bar.

# Examples

Pyridine, 0.0% err vs. exp: 35090 J/mol; from Poling [^footnote-2].

```pycon
>>> Riedel(388.4, 620.0, 56.3E5)
35089.80179000598
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Riedel, L. "Eine Neue Universelle Dampfdruckformel Untersuchungen
    Uber Eine Erweiterung Des Theorems Der Ubereinstimmenden Zustande. Teil
    I." Chemie Ingenieur Technik 26, no. 2 (February 1, 1954): 83-89.
    doi:10.1002/cite.330260206.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-3]: Green, Don, and Robert Perry. Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook,
    Eighth Edition. McGraw-Hill Professional, 2007.
## Enthalpy of Vaporization at Normal Boiling Point.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Chen
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization using the Chen [^footnote-1] correlation
and a chemical's critical temperature, pressure and boiling point.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta H_{vb} = RT_b \frac{3.978 T_r - 3.958 + 1.555 \ln P_c}{1.07 - T_r}
$$

# Parameters

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

The formulation presented in the original article is similar, but uses
units of atm and calorie instead. The form in [^footnote-2] has adjusted for this.
A method for estimating enthalpy of vaporization at other conditions
has also been developed, but the article is unclear on its implementation.
Based on the Pitzer correlation.

Internal units: bar and K

# Examples

Same problem as in Perry's examples.

```pycon
>>> Chen(294.0, 466.0, 5.55E6)
26705.902558030946
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chen, N. H. "Generalized Correlation for Latent Heat of Vaporization."
    Journal of Chemical & Engineering Data 10, no. 2 (April 1, 1965): 207-10.
    doi:10.1021/je60025a047

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Liu
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point using
the Liu [^footnote-1] correlation, and a chemical's critical temperature, pressure
and boiling point.

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\Delta H_{vap} = RT_b \left[ \frac{T_b}{220}\right]^{0.0627} \frac{ (1-T_{br})^{0.38} \ln(P_c/P_A)}{1-T_{br} + 0.38 T_{br} \ln T_{br}}
$$

# Parameters

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization, [J/mol]

# Notes

This formulation can be adjusted for lower boiling points, due to the use
of a rationalized pressure relationship. The formulation is taken from
the original article.

A correction for alcohols and organic acids based on carbon number,
which only modifies the boiling point, is available but not implemented.

No sample calculations are available in the article.

Internal units: Pa and K

# Examples

Same problem as in Perry's examples

```pycon
>>> Liu(294.0, 466.0, 5.55E6)
26378.575260517395
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: LIU, ZHI-YONG. "Estimation of Heat of Vaporization of Pure Liquid at
    Its Normal Boiling Temperature." Chemical Engineering Communications
    184, no. 1 (February 1, 2001): 221-28. doi:10.1080/00986440108912849.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Vetere
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization at the boiling point, using the
Vetere [^footnote-1] CSP method. Required information are critical temperature
and pressure, and boiling point. Equation taken from [^footnote-2].

The enthalpy of vaporization is given by:

$$
\frac {\Delta H_{vap}}{RT_b} = \frac{\tau_b^{0.38} \left[ \ln P_c - 0.513 + \frac{0.5066}{P_cT_{br}^2}\right]} {\tau_b + F(1-\tau_b^{0.38})\ln T_{br}}
$$

# Parameters

Tb

: Boiling temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

F

: Constant for a fluid, [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization at the boiling point, [J/mol]

# Notes

The equation cannot be found in the original source. It is believed that a
second article is its source, or that DIPPR staff have altered the formulation.

Internal units of pressure are bar.

# Examples

Example as in [^footnote-2], p2-487; exp: 25.73

```pycon
>>> Vetere(294.0, 466.0, 5.55E6)
26363.43895706672
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Vetere, Alessandro. "Methods to Predict the Vaporization Enthalpies
    at the Normal Boiling Temperature of Pure Compounds Revisited."
    Fluid Phase Equilibria 106, no. 1-2 (May 1, 1995): 1-10.
    doi:10.1016/0378-3812(94)02627-D.

[^footnote-2]: Green, Don, and Robert Perry. Perry's Chemical Engineers' Handbook,
    Eighth Edition. McGraw-Hill Professional, 2007.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Watson
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization of a chemical at a temperature
using the known heat of vaporization at another temperature according to
the Watson [^footnote-1] [^footnote-2] correlation. This is an application of the
corresponding-states principle, with an emperical temperature dependence.

$$
\frac{\Delta H_{vap}^{T1}}{\Delta H_{vap}^{T2}}  = \left( \frac{1-T_{r,1}}{1-T_{r,2}} \right)^{0.38}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature for which to calculate heat of vaporization, [K]

Hvap_ref

: Enthalpy of vaporization at the known temperature point, [J/mol]

T_ref

: Reference temperature; ideally as close to `T` as posible, [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

exponent

: A fit exponent can optionally be used instead of the Watson 0.38
  exponent, [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization at `T`, [J/mol]

# Notes

# Examples

Predict the enthalpy of vaporization of water at 320 K from a point at
300 K:

```pycon
>>> Watson(T=320, Hvap_ref=43908, T_ref=300.0, Tc=647.14)
42928.990094915454
```

The error is 0.38% compared to the correct value of 43048 J/mol.

If the provided temperature is above the critical point, zero is returned.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Watson, KM. "Thermodynamics of the Liquid State." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry 35, no. 4 (1943): 398-406.

[^footnote-2]: Martin, Joseph J., and John B. Edwards. "Correlation of Latent Heats
    of Vaporization.” AIChE Journal 11, no. 2 (1965): 331-33.
    <https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.690110226>.
## Enthalpy of Vaporization adjusted for T

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Watson_n
documentation:
Calculates the Watson heat of vaporizaton extrapolation exponent
given two known heats of vaporization.

$$
n = \left[ \frac{\ln{\left(\frac{Hvap_{1}}{Hvap_{2}} \right)}} {\ln{\left(\frac{T_{1} - T_{c}}{T_{2} - T_{c}} \right)}}\right]
$$

# Parameters

T1

: Temperature of first heat of vaporization point, [K]

T2

: Temperature of second heat of vaporization point, [K]

Hvap1

: Enthalpy of vaporization at the first known temperature point, [J/mol]

Hvap2

: Enthalpy of vaporization at the second known temperature point, [J/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

# Returns

exponent

: A fit exponent that can be used instead of the Watson 0.38 exponent,
  [-]

# Notes

This can be useful for extrapolating when a correlation does not reach
the critical point.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Watson_n(T1=320, T2=300, Hvap1=42928.990094915454, Hvap2=43908, Tc=647.14)
0.380000000000
```

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Alibakhshi
documentation:
Calculates enthalpy of vaporization of a chemical at a temperature
using a theoretically-derived single-coefficient fit equation developed in
[^footnote-1]. This model falls apart at ~0.8 Tc.

$$
\Delta H_{vap} = \left(4.5\pi N_A\right)^{1/3.}4.2\times 10^{-7} (T_c - 6) - 0.5RT\ln(T) + CT
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature for which to calculate heat of vaporization, [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

C

: Alibakhshi fit coefficient, [J/mol/K]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization at `T`, [J/mol]

# Notes

The authors of [^footnote-1] evaluated their model on 1890 compounds for a
temperature range of 50 K under `Tb` to 100 K below `Tc`, and obtained an
average absolute relative error of 4.5%.

# Examples

Predict the enthalpy of vaporization of water at 320 K:

```pycon
>>> Alibakhshi(T=320.0, Tc=647.14, C=-16.7171)
41961.30490225752
```

The error is 2.5% compared to the correct value of 43048 J/mol.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Alibakhshi, Amin. "Enthalpy of Vaporization, Its Temperature
    Dependence and Correlation with Surface Tension: A Theoretical Approach."
    Fluid Phase Equilibria 432 (January 25, 2017): 62-69.
    <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fluid.2016.10.013>.
## Enthalpy of Vaporization model equations

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.PPDS12
documentation:
Calculate the enthalpy of vaporization of a fluid using the 5-term
power fit developed by the PPDS and named PPDS equation 12.

$$
H_{vap} = RT_c \left(A\tau^{1/3} + B\tau^{2/3} + C\tau + D\tau^2 + E\tau^6\right)
$$

$$
\tau = 1 - \frac{T}{T_c}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

A

: Coefficient, [-]

B

: Coefficient, [-]

C

: Coefficient, [-]

D

: Coefficient, [-]

E

: Coefficient, [-]

# Returns

Hvap

: Enthalpy of vaporization at `T`, [J/mol]

# Notes

Coefficients can be found in [^footnote-1], but no other source for these
coefficients has been found.

# Examples

Example from [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> PPDS12(300.0, 591.75, 4.60584, 13.97224, -10.592315, 2.120205, 4.277128)
37948.76862035925
```

Example from [^footnote-2] for benzene; note the coefficients from [^footnote-2] predict
enthalpy of vaporization in kJ/mol, so the output must be adjusted. The same
effect can be obtained by multiplying each of the coefficients by 1000.

```pycon
>>> 1000.0*PPDS12(300.0, 562.05, 0.00171484, 0.0258604, -0.0243564, 0.00740881, 0.00680068)
33662.4258030
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gesellschaft, V. D. I., ed. VDI Heat Atlas. 2nd edition.
    Berlin; New York:: Springer, 2010.

[^footnote-2]: "Enthalpy of Vaporization: PPDS12."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/TDE_Help/Eqns-Pure-Hvap/PPDS12.htm>.

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Hfus_methods
documentation:
Return all methods available to obtain the heat of fusion for the
desired chemical.

Parameters
----------
CASRN : str
    CASRN, [-]

Returns
-------
methods : list[str]
    Methods which can be used to obtain the Hfus with the given inputs.

See Also
--------
Hfus

functions.name: chemicals.phase_change.Hfus
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval of a chemical's heat of fusion.
Lookup is based on CASRNs. Will automatically select a data
source to use if no method is provided; returns None if the data is not
available.

Function has data for approximately 22000 chemicals.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

Hfus

: Molar enthalpy of fusion at normal melting point, [J/mol]

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined by the variable,
  `Hfus_all_methods`.

# Notes

The available sources are as follows:

> - 'CRC', a compillation of data on organics and inorganics as published
>   in [^footnote-1].
> - 'WEBBOOK', a NIST resource [^footnote-4] containing mostly experimental
>   and averaged values
> - 'WIKIDATA', data from the Wikidata project [^footnote-2]
> - 'JOBACK', an estimation method for organic substances in [^footnote-3]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Hfus('7732-18-5')
6010.0
```

# See Also

Hfus_methods

# References

[^footnote-1]: Haynes, W.M., Thomas J. Bruno, and David R. Lide. CRC Handbook of
    Chemistry and Physics, 95E. Boca Raton, FL: CRC press, 2014.

[^footnote-2]: Wikidata. Wikidata. Accessed via API. <https://www.wikidata.org/>

[^footnote-3]: Joback, K.G., and R.C. Reid. "Estimation of Pure-Component
    Properties from Group-Contributions." Chemical Engineering
    Communications 57, no. 1-6 (July 1, 1987): 233-43.
    doi:10.1080/00986448708960487.

[^footnote-4]: Shen, V.K., Siderius, D.W., Krekelberg, W.P., and Hatch, H.W., Eds.,
    NIST WebBook, NIST, <http://doi.org/10.18434/T4M88Q>

classes.name: chemicals.exceptions.UnderspecifiedError
documentation: Generic error to raise when not enough values are given.

classes.name: chemicals.exceptions.OverspeficiedError
documentation: Generic error to raise when too many values are given.

classes.name: chemicals.exceptions.TrivialSolutionError
documentation: Error raised SS converges to trivial solution.

classes.name: chemicals.exceptions.PhaseCountReducedError
documentation: Error raised SS inner flash loop says all Ks are under 1 or above 1.

classes.name: chemicals.exceptions.PhaseExistenceImpossible
documentation: Error raised SS inner flash loop says all Ks are under 1 or above 1.
