functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.check_CAS
documentation:
Checks if a CAS number is valid. Returns False if the parser cannot parse
the given string.

# Parameters

CASRN

: A three-piece, dash-separated set of numbers

# Returns

result

: Boolean value if CASRN was valid. If parsing fails, return False also.

# Notes

Check method is according to Chemical Abstract Society. However, no lookup
to their service is performed; therefore, this function cannot detect
false positives.

Function also does not support additional separators, apart from '-'.

CAS numbers up to the series 1 XXX XXX-XX-X are now being issued.

A long can hold CAS numbers up to 2 147 483-64-7

# Examples

```pycon
>>> check_CAS('7732-18-5')
True
>>> check_CAS('77332-18-5')
False
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.CAS_to_int
documentation:
Converts CAS number of a compounds from a string to an int. This is
helpful when storing large amounts of CAS numbers, as their strings take up
more memory than their numerical representational. All CAS numbers fit into
64 bit ints.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Notes

Accomplishes conversion by removing dashes only, and then converting to an
int. An incorrect CAS number will change without exception.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> CAS_to_int('7704-34-9')
7704349
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.int_to_CAS
documentation:
Converts CAS number of a compounds from an int to an string. This is
helpful when dealing with int CAS numbers.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Notes

Handles CAS numbers with an unspecified number of digits. Does not work on
floats.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> int_to_CAS(7704349)
'7704-34-9'
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.sorted_CAS_key
documentation:
Takes a list of CAS numbers as strings, and returns a tuple of the same
CAS numbers, sorted from smallest to largest. This is very convenient for
obtaining a unique hash of a set of compounds, so as to see if two
groups of compounds are the same.

# Parameters

CASs

: CAS numbers as strings [-]

# Returns

CASs_sorted

: Sorted CAS numbers from lowest (first) to highest (last) [-]

# Notes

Does not check CAS numbers for validity.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sorted_CAS_key(['7732-18-5', '64-17-5', '108-88-3', '98-00-0'])
('64-17-5', '98-00-0', '108-88-3', '7732-18-5')
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.CAS_from_any
documentation:
Wrapper around `search_chemical` which returns the CAS number of the
found chemical directly.

# Parameters

ID

: One of the name formats described by `search_chemical`, [-]

autoload

: Whether to load new chemical databanks during the search if a hit is
  not immediately found, [-]

cache

: Whether or not to cache the search for faster lookup in subsequent
  queries, [-]

# Returns

CASRN

: A three-piece, dash-separated set of numbers

# Notes

An exception is raised if the name cannot be identified. The PubChem
database includes a wide variety of other synonyms, but these may not be
present for all chemcials. See `search_chemical` for more details.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> CAS_from_any('water')
'7732-18-5'
>>> CAS_from_any('InChI=1S/C2H6O/c1-2-3/h3H,2H2,1H3')
'64-17-5'
>>> CAS_from_any('CCCCCCCCCC')
'124-18-5'
>>> CAS_from_any('InChIKey=LFQSCWFLJHTTHZ-UHFFFAOYSA-N')
'64-17-5'
>>> CAS_from_any('pubchem=702')
'64-17-5'
>>> CAS_from_any('O') # only elements can be specified by symbol
'17778-80-2'
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.MW
documentation:
Wrapper around `search_chemical` which returns the molecular weight of the
found chemical directly.

# Parameters

ID

: One of the name formats described by `search_chemical`

# Returns

MW

: Molecular weight of chemical, [g/mol]

# Notes

An exception is raised if the name cannot be identified. The PubChem
database includes a wide variety of other synonyms, but these may not be
present for all chemcials. See `search_chemical` for more details.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> MW('water')
18.01528
>>> MW('InChI=1S/C2H6O/c1-2-3/h3H,2H2,1H3')
46.06844
>>> MW('CCCCCCCCCC')
142.28168
>>> MW('InChIKey=LFQSCWFLJHTTHZ-UHFFFAOYSA-N')
46.06844
>>> MW('pubchem=702')
46.06844
>>> MW('O') # only elements can be specified by symbol
15.9994
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.search_chemical
documentation:
Looks up metadata about a chemical by searching and testing for the input
string being any of the following types of chemical identifiers:

- Name, in IUPAC form or common form or a synonym registered in PubChem
- InChI name, prefixed by 'InChI=1S/' or 'InChI=1/'
- InChI key, prefixed by 'InChIKey='
- PubChem CID, prefixed by 'PubChem='
- SMILES (prefix with 'SMILES=' to ensure smiles parsing; ex.
  'C' will return Carbon as it is an element whereas the SMILES
  interpretation for 'C' is methane)
- CAS number (obsolete numbers may point to the current number)

If the input is an ID representing an element, the following additional
inputs may be specified as

- Atomic symbol (ex 'Na')
- Atomic number (as a string)

# Parameters

ID

: One of the name formats described above

autoload

: Whether to load new chemical databanks during the search if a hit is
  not immediately found, [-]

cache

: Whether or not to cache the search for faster lookup in subsequent
  queries, [-]

# Returns

chemical_metadata

: A class containing attributes which describe the chemical's metadata,
  [-]

# Notes

An exception is raised if the name cannot be identified. The PubChem
database includes a wide variety of other synonyms, but these may not be
present for all chemcials.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> search_chemical('water')
<ChemicalMetadata, name=water, formula=H2O, smiles=O, MW=18.0153>
>>> search_chemical('InChI=1S/C2H6O/c1-2-3/h3H,2H2,1H3')
<ChemicalMetadata, name=ethanol, formula=C2H6O, smiles=CCO, MW=46.0684>
>>> search_chemical('CCCCCCCCCC')
<ChemicalMetadata, name=decane, formula=C10H22, smiles=CCCCCCCCCC, MW=142.282>
>>> search_chemical('InChIKey=LFQSCWFLJHTTHZ-UHFFFAOYSA-N')
<ChemicalMetadata, name=ethanol, formula=C2H6O, smiles=CCO, MW=46.0684>
>>> search_chemical('pubchem=702')
<ChemicalMetadata, name=ethanol, formula=C2H6O, smiles=CCO, MW=46.0684>
>>> search_chemical('O') # only elements can be specified by symbol
<ChemicalMetadata, name=oxygen, formula=O, smiles=[O], MW=15.9994>
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.dippr_compounds
documentation:
Loads and returns a set of compounds known in the DIPPR database. This
can be useful for knowing if a chemical is of industrial relevance.

Returns
-------
dippr_compounds : set([str])
    A set of CAS numbers from the 2014 edition of the DIPPR database.
## DIPPR Database, chemical list only
Obtained via the command:
list(pd.read_excel('http://www.aiche.org/sites/default/files/docs/pages/sponsor_compound_list-2014.xlsx')['Unnamed: 2'])[2:]
This is consistently faster than creating a list and then making the set.

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.mixture_from_any
documentation:
Search by string for a mixture in the included common mixture database.
The database primarily contains refrigerant blends. The variable
`common_mixtures` contains all loaded entries.

# Parameters

ID

: A string or 1-element list containing the name which may represent a
  mixture.

# Returns

mixture

: Object containing basic mixture information

# Notes

White space, '-', and upper case letters are removed in the search.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> mixture_from_any('R512A')
<MixtureMetadata, name=R512A, N=2, CASs=['811-97-2', '75-37-6'], ws=[0.05, 0.95], zs=[0.032949, 0.96705]>
>>> mixture_from_any(['air'])
<MixtureMetadata, name=Air, N=3, CASs=['7727-37-9', '7440-37-1', '7782-44-7'], ws=[0.7557, 0.0127, 0.2316], zs=[0.7812, 0.0092, 0.2096]>
```

functions.name: chemicals.identifiers.IDs_to_CASs
documentation:
Find the CAS numbers for multiple chemicals names at once. Also supports
having a string input which is a common mixture name in the database.
An error will be raised if any of the chemicals cannot be found.

# Parameters

IDs

: A string or 1-element list containing the name which may represent a
  mixture.

# Returns

CASs

: CAS numbers of found chemicals, [-]

# Notes

White space, '-', and upper case letters are removed in the search.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> IDs_to_CASs('R512A')
['811-97-2', '75-37-6']
>>> IDs_to_CASs(['norflurane', '1,1-difluoroethane'])
['811-97-2', '75-37-6']
```

methods.name: chemicals.identifiers.ChemicalMetadata.CASs
documentation: CAs number of the compound as a string.

Class for storing metadata on chemicals.

# Attributes

pubchemid

: Identification number on pubchem database; access their information
  online at <https://pubchem.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/compound>/\<pubchemid>
  [-]

formula

: Formula of the compound; in the same format as
  {obj}`chemicals.elements.serialize_formula` generates, [-]

MW

: Molecular weight of the compound as calculated with the standard
  atomic abundances; consistent with the element weights in
  {obj}`chemicals.elements.periodic_table`, [g/mol]

smiles

: SMILES identification string, [-]

InChI

: InChI identification string as given in pubchem (there can be multiple
  valid InChI strings for a compound), [-]

InChI_key

: InChI key identification string (meant to be unique to a compound), [-]

iupac_name

: IUPAC name as given in pubchem, [-]

common_name

: Common name as given in pubchem, [-]

synonyms

: List of synonyms of the compound, [-]

CAS

: CAS number of the compound; stored as an int for memory efficiency, [-]

modules.name: chemicals.safety
documentation:
Chemical Engineering Design Library (ChEDL). Utilities for process modeling.
Copyright (C) 2016, Caleb Bell \<<mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>>

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

This module contains functions for lookup the following properties for a
chemical:

- Short-term Exposure Limit (STEL)
- Time-Weighted Average Exposure Limit (TWA)
- Celing limit for working exposure
- Whether a chemicals is absorbed thorough human skin
- Whether a chemical is a carcinogen, suspected of being a carcinogen, or has
  been identified as unlikely to be a carcinogen
- Flash point
- Auto ignition point
- Lower flammability limit
- Upper flammability limit

In addition, several estimation methods for chemicals without flammability
limits are provided and for calculating the flammability limits of mixtures.

This module also contains several utility functions.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals/).


functions.name: chemicals.safety.ppmv_to_mgm3
documentation:
Converts a concentration in ppmv to units of mg/m^3. Used in
industrial toxicology.

$$
\frac{mg}{m^3} = \frac{ppmv\cdot P}{RT}\cdot \frac{MW}{1000}
$$

# Parameters

ppmv

: Concentration of a component in a gas mixure [parts per million,
  volumetric]

MW

: Molecular weight of the trace gas [g/mol]

T

: Temperature of the gas at which the ppmv is reported, [K]

P

: Pressure of the gas at which the ppmv is reported, [Pa]

# Returns

mgm3

: Concentration of a substance in an ideal gas mixture [mg/m^3]

# Notes

The term P/(RT)/1000 converts to 0.040874 at STP. Its inverse is reported
as 24.45 in [^footnote-1].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> ppmv_to_mgm3(1.0, 40.0)
1.6349617809430446
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ACGIH. Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice,
    23rd Edition. American Conference of Governmental and Industrial
    Hygenists, 2004.
## Utilities

functions.name: chemicals.safety.mgm3_to_ppmv
documentation:
Converts a concentration in mg/m^3 to units of ppmv. Used in
industrial toxicology.

$$
ppmv = \frac{1000RT}{MW\cdot P} \cdot \frac{mg}{m^3}
$$

# Parameters

mgm3

: Concentration of a substance in an ideal gas mixture [mg/m^3]

MW

: Molecular weight of the trace gas [g/mol]

T

: Temperature of the gas at which the ppmv is reported, [K]

P

: Pressure of the gas at which the ppmv is reported, [Pa]

# Returns

ppmv

: Concentration of a component in a gas mixure [parts per million,
  volumetric]

# Notes

The term P/(RT)/1000 converts to 0.040874 at STP. Its inverse is reported
as 24.45 in [^footnote-1].

# Examples

```pycon
>>> mgm3_to_ppmv(1.635, 40.0)
1.0000233761164334
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: ACGIH. Industrial Ventilation: A Manual of Recommended Practice,
    23rd Edition. American Conference of Governmental and Industrial
    Hygenists, 2004.

functions.name: chemicals.safety.TWA_methods
documentation:
Return all methods available to obtain the Time-Weighted Average exposure
limits (TWA) for the desired chemical.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN, [-]

# Returns

methods

: Methods which can be used to obtain TWA with the given inputs.

# See Also

TWA

# Examples

```pycon
>>> TWA_methods('71-43-2')
['Ontario Limits']
```

functions.name: chemicals.safety.TWA
documentation:
Return the Time-Weighted Average exposure
limits (TWA) for the desired chemical if it is available.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN, [-]

method

: Name of method to use, [-]

# Returns

TWA

: Time-Weighted Average exposure, [ppm or mg/m^3]

units

: One of ppm or mg/m^3, [-]

# Notes

The ppm value is preferentially returned if both are available. While they
can be converted in specific cases, it is better to work with the specified
units of the original source.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> TWA('98-00-0')
(10.0, 'ppm')
>>> TWA('1303-00-0')
(5.0742430905659505e-05, 'ppm')
```

functions.name: chemicals.safety.STEL
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval of Short-term Exposure Limit (STEL)
on worker exposure to dangerous chemicals.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN, [-]

method

: Name of method to use, [-]

# Returns

STEL

: Short-term Exposure Limit, [ppm or mg/m^3]

units

: One of ppm or mg/m^3, [-]

# Notes

The ppm value is preferentially returned if both are available. While they
can be converted in specific cases, it is better to work with the specified
units of the original source.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> STEL('67-64-1')
(750.0, 'ppm')
>>> STEL('7664-38-2')
(0.7489774978301237, 'ppm')
>>> STEL('55720-99-5')
(2.0, 'mg/m^3')
```

Return all methods available to obtain Carcinogen listings for the
desired chemical.

Carcinogen

functions.name: chemicals.safety.Carcinogen
documentation:
Looks up if a chemical is listed as a carcinogen or not according to
either a specifc method or with all methods.
Returns either the status as a string for a specified method, or the
status of the chemical in all available data sources, in the format
{source: status}.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

status

: Carcinogen status information [-].

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined in the variable,
  `Carcinogen_all_methods`.

# Notes

Supported methods are:
: - **IARC**: International Agency for Research on Cancer, [^footnote-1]. As
    extracted with a last update of February 22, 2016. Has listing
    information of 863 chemicals with CAS numbers. Chemicals without
    CAS numbers not included here. If two listings for the same CAS
    were available, the harshest rating was used. If two
    listings were available published at different times, the latest
    value was used. All else equal, the most pessimistic value was used.
  - **NTP**: National Toxicology Program, [^footnote-2]. Has data on 228
    chemicals.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Carcinogen('61-82-5')
{'International Agency for Research on Cancer': 'Not classifiable as to its carcinogenicity to humans (3)', 'National Toxicology Program 13th Report on Carcinogens': 'Reasonably Anticipated'}
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: International Agency for Research on Cancer. Agents Classified by
    the IARC Monographs, Volumes 1-115. Lyon, France: IARC; 2020 Available
    from: <http://monographs.iarc.fr/ENG/Classification/>

[^footnote-2]: NTP (National Toxicology Program). 2021. Report on Carcinogens,
    Fifteenth Edition.; Research Triangle Park, NC: U.S. Department of
    Health and Human Services, Public Health Service.
    <https://doi.org/10.22427/NTP-OTHER-1003>

functions.name: chemicals.safety.T_flash_methods
documentation:
Return all methods available to obtain T_flash for the desired chemical.

Parameters

Returns

See Also

T_flash

functions.name: chemicals.safety.T_flash
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval or calculation of a chemical's
flash point. Lookup is based on CASRNs. No predictive methods are currently
implemented. Will automatically select a data source to use if no method
is provided; returns None if the data is not available.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> T_flash(CASRN='64-17-5')
285.15
>>> T_flash('111-69-3', method='WIKIDATA')
365.92778
```

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

T_flash

: Flash point of the chemical, [K]

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined in the variable,
  `T_flash_all_methods`,

# Notes

Preferred source is 'IEC 60079-20-1 (2010)' [^footnote-1], with the secondary source
'NFPA 497 (2008)' [^footnote-2] having very similar data. A third source
'Serat DIPPR (2017)' [^footnote-3] provides third hand experimental but evaluated
data from the DIPPR database, version unspecified, for 870 compounds.

The predicted values from the DIPPR databank are also available in the
supporting material in [^footnote-3], but are not included.

# See Also

T_flash_methods

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC. "IEC 60079-20-1:2010 Explosive atmospheres - Part 20-1:
    Material characteristics for gas and vapour classification - Test
    methods and data." <https://webstore.iec.ch/publication/635>. See also
    <https://law.resource.org/pub/in/bis/S05/is.iec.60079.20.1.2010.pdf>

[^footnote-2]: National Fire Protection Association. NFPA 497: Recommended
    Practice for the Classification of Flammable Liquids, Gases, or Vapors
    and of Hazardous. NFPA, 2008.

[^footnote-3]: Serat, Fatima Zohra, Ali Mustapha Benkouider, Ahmed Yahiaoui, and
    Farid Bagui. "Nonlinear Group Contribution Model for the Prediction of
    Flash Points Using Normal Boiling Points." Fluid Phase Equilibria 449
    (October 15, 2017): 52-59. doi:10.1016/j.fluid.2017.06.008.

[^footnote-4]: Wikidata. Wikidata. Accessed via API. <https://www.wikidata.org/>

functions.name: chemicals.safety.T_autoignition_methods

functions.name: chemicals.safety.T_autoignition
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval or calculation of a chemical's
autoifnition temperature. Lookup is based on CASRNs. No predictive methods
are currently implemented. Will automatically select a data source to use
if no Method is provided; returns None if the data is not available.

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

# Returns

Tautoignition

: Autoignition point of the chemical, [K].

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined in the variable,
  `T_autoignition_all_methods`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> T_autoignition(CASRN='71-43-2')
771.15
>>> T_autoignition('111-69-3', method='WIKIDATA')
823.15
```

# Notes

Preferred source is 'IEC 60079-20-1 (2010)' [^footnote-1], with the secondary source
'NFPA 497 (2008)' [^footnote-2] having very similar data.

# See Also

T_autoignition_methods

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC. “IEC 60079-20-1:2010 Explosive atmospheres - Part 20-1:
    Material characteristics for gas and vapour classification - Test
    methods and data.” <https://webstore.iec.ch/publication/635>. See also
    <https://law.resource.org/pub/in/bis/S05/is.iec.60079.20.1.2010.pdf>

[^footnote-2]: National Fire Protection Association. NFPA 497: Recommended
    Practice for the Classification of Flammable Liquids, Gases, or Vapors
    and of Hazardous. NFPA, 2008.

[^footnote-3]: Wikidata. Wikidata. Accessed via API. <https://www.wikidata.org/>

functions.name: chemicals.safety.LFL_methods
documentation:
Return all methods available to obtain LFL for the desired chemical.

# Parameters

Hc

: Heat of combustion of gas [J/mol].

atoms

: Dictionary of atoms and atom counts.

CASRN

: CASRN, [-]

# Returns

methods

: Methods which can be used to obtain LFL with the given inputs.

# See Also

LFL

# Examples

Methane

```pycon
>>> LFL_methods(Hc=-890590.0, atoms={'C': 1, 'H': 4}, CASRN='74-82-8')
```

functions.name: chemicals.safety.LFL
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval or calculation of a chemical's
Lower Flammability Limit. Lookup is based on CASRNs. Will automatically
select a data source to use if no Method is provided; returns None if the
data is not available.

# Parameters

Hc

: Heat of combustion of gas [J/mol].

atoms

: Dictionary of atoms and atom counts.

CASRN

: CASRN, [-]

# Returns

LFL

: Lower flammability limit of the gas in an atmosphere at STP, [mole fraction].

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined in the variable,
  `LFL_all_methods`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> LFL(CASRN='71-43-2')
0.012
>>> LFL(Hc=-890590.0, atoms={'C': 1, 'H': 4}, CASRN='74-82-8')
0.044
>>> LFL(CASRN='111-69-3', method='WIKIDATA')
0.017
```

# Notes

Preferred source is 'IEC 60079-20-1 (2010)' [^footnote-1], with the secondary source
'NFPA 497 (2008)' [^footnote-2] having very similar data. If the heat of combustion
is provided, the estimation method {obj}`Suzuki_LFL` can be used. If the atoms
of the molecule are available, the method {obj}`Crowl_Louvar_LFL` can be used.

# References

[^footnote-1]: IEC. “IEC 60079-20-1:2010 Explosive atmospheres - Part 20-1:
    Material characteristics for gas and vapour classification - Test
    methods and data.” <https://webstore.iec.ch/publication/635>. See also
    <https://law.resource.org/pub/in/bis/S05/is.iec.60079.20.1.2010.pdf>

[^footnote-2]: National Fire Protection Association. NFPA 497: Recommended
    Practice for the Classification of Flammable Liquids, Gases, or Vapors
    and of Hazardous. NFPA, 2008.

[^footnote-3]: Wikidata. Wikidata. Accessed via API. <https://www.wikidata.org/>

```pycon
>>> UFL(Hc=-890590.0, atoms={'C': 1, 'H': 4}, CASRN='74-82-8')

>>> UFL(CASRN='111-69-3', method='WIKIDATA')
```

functions.name: chemicals.safety.LFL_ISO_10156_2017
documentation:
Calculate the lower flammability limit of a mixture of combustible gases
and inert gases according to ISO 10156 (2017) [^footnote-1].

$$
\text{LFL} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n_{combustible}}\frac{A_i}{\text{LFL}_i'}}
$$

$$
\text{LFL}_i' = \frac{1 - \text{LFL}_m' - (1 - K) \frac{\sum_j^{n_{inert}} B_j}{\sum_j^{n_{combustible}} A_j} \text{LFL}_m' } {100 - \text{LFL}_m'}\text{LFL}_i
$$

$$
K = \sum_i^{n_{inert}} z_i K_k
$$

The `B` sum is the total mole fraction of all inert gas compounds;
and the `A` sum is the total mole fraction of all combustible compounds.
$K_k$ are the looked up inert gas coefficients.
$\text{LFL}_m'$ is calculated as the Le Chatelier's lower
flammability limit if there were no inert gases in the mixture.

# Parameters

zs

: Mole fractions of all components in a gas including inerts, [-]

LFLs

: Lower or upper flammability limits for each flammable component in a
  gas, [-]

CASs

: CAS numbers of each compound; required to look up inert gas factors, [-]

# Returns

LFL

: Lower or flammability limit of a gas mixture, [-]

# Notes

Inert gas parameters are available for O2, N2, CO2, He, Ar, Ne, Kr, Xe,
SO2, SF6, CF4, C3F8, and C2HF5.

# Examples

All the sample problems from [^footnote-1] have been implemented as tests.

```pycon
>>> zs = [.15, .15, .3, .35+.05*.79, .05*.21]
>>> LFLs = [.04, .044, None, None, None]
>>> CASs = ['1333-74-0', '74-82-8', '124-38-9', '7727-37-9', '7782-44-7']
>>> LFL_ISO_10156_2017(zs, LFLs, CASs)
0.1427372274
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Standardization, International Organization for. ISO 10156: 2017 :
    Gas Cylinders - Gases and Gas Mixtures - Determination of Fire Potential
    and Oxidizing Ability for the Selection of Cylinder Valve Outlets, 2017.

functions.name: chemicals.safety.fire_mixing
documentation:
Le Chatelier's mixing rule for lower and upper flammability limits of
mixtures of gases.

# Parameters

ys

: Normalized mole fractions of all flammable components in a gas, [-]

FLs

: Lower or upper flammability limits for each flammable component in a
  gas, [-]

# Returns

FL

: Lower or upper flammability limit of a gas, [-]

# Notes

This equation has a higher accuracy for lower flammability limits than
upper flammability limits. Some sources recommend not using it for
upper flammability limits.

# Examples

Sample problems from [^footnote-1] for the lower and upper flammability limit.

```pycon
>>> fire_mixing(ys=normalize([0.0024, 0.0061, 0.0015]), FLs=[.012, .053, .031])
0.02751172136637642
```

```pycon
>>> fire_mixing(ys=normalize([0.0024, 0.0061, 0.0015]), FLs=[.075, .15, .32])
0.12927551844869378
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Crowl, Daniel A., and Joseph F. Louvar. Chemical Process Safety:
    Fundamentals with Applications. 2E. Upper Saddle River, N.J: Prentice
    Hall, 2001.

functions.name: chemicals.safety.Suzuki_LFL
documentation:
Calculates lower flammability limit, using the Suzuki [^footnote-1] correlation.
Uses heat of combustion only.

The lower flammability limit of a gas is air is:

$$
\text{LFL} = \frac{-3.42}{\Delta H_c^{\circ}} + 0.569
$$

$$
\Delta H_c^{\circ} + 0.0538\Delta H_c^{\circ 2} + 1.80
$$

# Parameters

Hc

: Heat of combustion of gas [J/mol]

# Returns

LFL

: Lower flammability limit, mole fraction [-]

# Notes

Fit performed with 112 compounds, r^2 was 0.977.
LFL in percent volume in air. Hc is at standard conditions, in MJ/mol.
11 compounds left out as they were outliers.
Equation does not apply for molecules with halogen atoms, only hydrocarbons
with oxygen or nitrogen or sulfur.
No sample calculation provided with the article. However, the equation is
straightforward.
Limits of equations's validity are -6135596 J where it predicts a
LFL of 0, and -48322129 J where it predicts a LFL of 1.

# Examples

Pentane, 1.5 % LFL in literature

```pycon
>>> Suzuki_LFL(-3536600)
0.014276107095811815
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Suzuki, Takahiro. "Note: Empirical Relationship between Lower
    Flammability Limits and Standard Enthalpies of Combustion of Organic
    Compounds." Fire and Materials 18, no. 5 (September 1, 1994): 333-36.
    doi:10.1002/fam.810180509.

functions.name: chemicals.safety.Suzuki_UFL
documentation:
Calculates upper flammability limit, using the Suzuki [^footnote-1] correlation.
Uses heat of combustion only.
The upper flammability limit of a gas is air is:

$$
\text{UFL} = 6.3\Delta H_c^\circ + 0.567\Delta H_c^{\circ 2} + 23.5
$$

# Parameters

Hc

: Heat of combustion of gas [J/mol]

# Returns

UFL

: Upper flammability limit, mole fraction

# Notes

UFL in percent volume in air according to original equation.
Hc is at standard conditions in the equation, in units of MJ/mol.
AAPD = 1.2% for 95 compounds used in fit.
Somewhat better results than the High and Danner method.
4.9% < UFL < 23.0%
-890.3 kJ/mol < dHc < -6380 kJ/mol
r^2 = 0.989
Sample calculations provided for all chemicals, both this method and
High and Danner. Examples are from the article.
Predicts a UFL of 1 at 7320190 J and a UFL of 0 at -5554160 J.

# Examples

Pentane, literature 7.8% UFL

```pycon
>>> Suzuki_UFL(-3536600)
0.0831119493052
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Suzuki, Takahiro, and Kozo Koide. "Short Communication: Correlation
    between Upper Flammability Limits and Thermochemical Properties of
    Organic Compounds." Fire and Materials 18, no. 6 (November 1, 1994):
    393-97. doi:10.1002/fam.810180608.

functions.name: chemicals.safety.Crowl_Louvar_LFL
documentation:
Calculates lower flammability limit, using the Crowl-Louvar [^footnote-1]
correlation. Uses molecular formula only.
The lower flammability limit of a gas is air is:

$$
C_mH_xO_y + zO_2 \to mCO_2 + \frac{x}{2}H_2O
$$

$$
\text{LFL} = \frac{0.55}{4.76m + 1.19x - 2.38y + 1}
$$

# Parameters

atoms

: Dictionary of atoms and atom counts

# Returns

LFL

: Lower flammability limit, mole fraction

# Notes

Coefficient of 0.55 taken from [^footnote-2]

# Examples

Hexane, example from [^footnote-1], lit. 1.2 %

```pycon
>>> Crowl_Louvar_LFL({'H': 14, 'C': 6})
0.011899610558199915
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Crowl, Daniel A., and Joseph F. Louvar. Chemical Process Safety:
    Fundamentals with Applications. 2E. Upper Saddle River, N.J:
    Prentice Hall, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: Jones, G. W. "Inflammation Limits and Their Practical Application
    in Hazardous Industrial Operations." Chemical Reviews 22, no. 1
    (February 1, 1938): 1-26. doi:10.1021/cr60071a001

functions.name: chemicals.safety.Crowl_Louvar_UFL
documentation:
Calculates upper flammability limit, using the Crowl-Louvar [^footnote-1]
correlation. Uses molecular formula only.
The upper flammability limit of a gas is air is:

$$
C_mH_xO_y + zO_2 \to mCO_2 + \frac{x}{2}H_2O
$$

$$
\text{UFL} = \frac{3.5}{4.76m + 1.19x - 2.38y + 1}
$$

# Parameters

atoms

: Dictionary of atoms and atom counts

# Returns

UFL

: Upper flammability limit, mole fraction

# Notes

Coefficient of 3.5 taken from [^footnote-2]

# Examples

Hexane, example from [^footnote-1], lit. 7.5 %

```pycon
>>> Crowl_Louvar_UFL({'H': 14, 'C': 6})
0.07572479446127219
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Crowl, Daniel A., and Joseph F. Louvar. Chemical Process Safety:
    Fundamentals with Applications. 2E. Upper Saddle River, N.J:
    Prentice Hall, 2001.

[^footnote-2]: Jones, G. W. "Inflammation Limits and Their Practical Application
    in Hazardous Industrial Operations." Chemical Reviews 22, no. 1
    (February 1, 1938): 1-26. doi:10.1021/cr60071a001

functions.name: chemicals.safety.NFPA_30_classification
documentation:
Classify a chemical's flammability/combustibility according
to the NFPA 30 standard Flammable and Combustible Liquids Code.

Class IA: Flash Point < 73°F; Boiling Point < 100°F
Class IB: Flash Point < 73°F; 100°F \<= Boiling Point
Class IC: 73°F \<= Flash Point < 100°F
Class II: 100°F \<= Flash Point < 140°F
Class IIIA: 140°F \<= Flash Point < 200°F
Class IIIB: 200°F \<= Flash Point

Class I liquids are designated as flammable; class II and II
liquids are designated as combustible.

# Parameters

T_flash

: Flash point (closed-cup method, adjusted for sea level), [K]

Tb

: Normal boiling point (needed to classify IA and IB liquids), [K]

Psat_100F

: Vapor pressure at 100°F (needed to classify IA and IB liquids), [K]

# Returns

classification

: One of 'IA', 'IB', 'IC', 'II', 'IIIA', 'IIIB', [-]

# Notes

Only one of `Tb` or `Psat_100F` is needed.

Class 'IA' also includes unstable liquids.

# Examples

Ethylene oxide

```pycon
>>> NFPA_30_classification(253.15, 283.55)
'IA'
```

Butyl alcohol

```pycon
>>> NFPA_30_classification(308.15)
'IC'
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: NFPA (National Fire Prevention Association). NFPA 30: Flammable and
    Combustible Liquids Code, 2008. National Fire Protection Association
    (NFPA), 2007.

documentation:
Chemical Engineering Design Library (ChEDL). Utilities for process modeling.
Copyright (C) 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020 Caleb Bell \<<mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>>

Permission is hereby granted, free of charge, to any person obtaining a copy

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

This module contains lookup functions and estimation methods for the
parameters molecular diameter `sigma` and the Stockmayer parameter `epsilon`.
These are used for diffusivity calculations. It also contains several
methods for computing the collision integral, another parameter used in the
Lennard-Jones model.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals/).


    CASRN [-]

Tb : float, optional
    Boiling temperature of compound [K]
Tc : float, optional
    Critical temperature of compound, [K]
Zc : float, optional
    Critical compressibility of compound, [-]

Stockmayer

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.Stockmayer
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval or calculation a chemical's
Stockmayer parameter. Values are available from one source with lookup
based on CASRNs, or can be estimated from 7 CSP methods.
Will automatically select a data source to use if no method is provided;
returns None if the data is not available.

Preferred sources are 'Magalhães, Lito, Da Silva, and Silva (2013)' for
common chemicals which had valies listed in that source, and the CSP method
`Tee, Gotoh, and Stewart CSP with Tc, omega (1966)` for chemicals which
don't.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Stockmayer(CASRN='64-17-5')
1291.41
>>> Stockmayer('7727-37-9')
71.4
```

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

Tm

: Melting temperature of compound [K]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of compound [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of compound, [K]

Zc

: Critical compressibility of compound, [-]

omega

: Acentric factor of compound, [-]

# Returns

epsilon_k

: Lennard-Jones depth of potential-energy minimum over k, [K]

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined by constants in
  Stockmayer_all_methods

# Notes

These values are somewhat rough, as they attempt to pigeonhole a chemical
into L-J behavior.

The tabulated data is from [^footnote-2], for 322 chemicals.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bird, R. Byron, Warren E. Stewart, and Edwin N. Lightfoot.
    Transport Phenomena, Revised 2nd Edition. New York:
    John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2006

[^footnote-2]: Magalhães, Ana L., Patrícia F. Lito, Francisco A. Da Silva, and
    Carlos M. Silva. "Simple and Accurate Correlations for Diffusion
    Coefficients of Solutes in Liquids and Supercritical Fluids over Wide
    Ranges of Temperature and Density." The Journal of Supercritical Fluids
    76 (April 2013): 94-114. doi:10.1016/j.supflu.2013.02.002.

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.molecular_diameter
documentation:
This function handles the retrieval or calculation a chemical's
L-J molecular diameter. Values are available from one source with lookup
based on CASRNs, or can be estimated from 9 CSP methods.
Will automatically select a data source to use if no method is provided;
returns None if the data is not available.

Preferred sources are 'Magalhães, Lito, Da Silva, and Silva (2013)' for
common chemicals which had valies listed in that source, and the CSP method
`Tee, Gotoh, and Stewart CSP with Tc, Pc, omega (1966)` for chemicals which
don't.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> molecular_diameter(CASRN='64-17-5')
4.23738
>>> molecular_diameter('7727-37-9')
3.798
```

# Parameters

CASRN

: CASRN [-]

Tc

: Critical temperature, [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure, [Pa]

Vc

: Critical volume, [m^3/mol]

Zc

: Critical compressibility, [-]

omega

: Acentric factor of compound, [-]

Vm

: Molar volume of liquid at the melting point of the fluid [K]

Vb

: Molar volume of liquid at the boiling point of the fluid [K]

# Returns

sigma

: Lennard-Jones molecular diameter, [Angstrom]

# Other Parameters

method

: A string for the method name to use, as defined by constants in
  molecular_diameter_all_methods

# Notes

These values are somewhat rough, as they attempt to pigeonhole a chemical
into L-J behavior.

The tabulated data is from [^footnote-2], for 322 chemicals.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bird, R. Byron, Warren E. Stewart, and Edwin N. Lightfoot.
    Transport Phenomena, Revised 2nd Edition. New York:
    John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2006

[^footnote-2]: Magalhães, Ana L., Patrícia F. Lito, Francisco A. Da Silva, and
    Carlos M. Silva. "Simple and Accurate Correlations for Diffusion
    Coefficients of Solutes in Liquids and Supercritical Fluids over Wide

    76 (April 2013): 94-114. doi:10.1016/j.supflu.2013.02.002.

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.sigma_Flynn
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones molecular diameter.
Uses critical volume. CSP method by [^footnote-1] as reported in [^footnote-2].

$$
\sigma = 0.561(V_c^{1/3})^{5/4}
$$

# Parameters

Vc

: Critical volume of fluid [m^3/mol]

# Returns

sigma

: Lennard-Jones molecular diameter, [Angstrom]

# Notes

Vc is originally in units of mL/mol.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sigma_Flynn(0.000268)
5.2506948422196285
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Flynn, L.W., M.S. thesis, Northwestern Univ., Evanston, Ill. (1960).

[^footnote-2]: Stiel, L. I., and George Thodos. "Lennard-Jones Force Constants
    Predicted from Critical Properties." Journal of Chemical & Engineering
    Data 7, no. 2 (April 1, 1962): 234-36. doi:10.1021/je60013a023
## Sigma Lennard-Jones

documentation:

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.sigma_Tee_Gotoh_Steward_1
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones molecular diameter.
Uses critical temperature and pressure. CSP method by [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma = 2.3647 \left(\frac{T_c}{P_c}\right)^{1/3}

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

sigma

Original units of Pc are atm. Further regressions with other parameters
were performed in [^footnote-1] but are not included here, except for

```pycon
>>> sigma_Tee_Gotoh_Steward_1(560.1, 4550000)
```

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.sigma_Tee_Gotoh_Steward_2
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones molecular diameter.
Uses critical temperature, pressure, and acentric factor. CSP method by
[^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma = (2.3551 - 0.0874\omega)\left(\frac{T_c}{P_c}\right)^{1/3}
$$

# Parameters

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Lennard-Jones molecular diameter, [Angstrom]

# Notes

Original units of Pc are atm. Further regressions with other parameters
were performed in [^footnote-1] but are not included here, except for
`sigma_Tee_Gotoh_Steward_1`.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sigma_Tee_Gotoh_Steward_2(560.1, 4550000, 0.245)
5.412104867264477
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Tee, L. S., Sukehiro Gotoh, and W. E. Stewart. "Molecular Parameters
    for Normal Fluids. Lennard-Jones 12-6 Potential." Industrial
    & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 5, no. 3 (August 1, 1966): 356-63.
    doi:10.1021/i160019a011

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.sigma_Silva_Liu_Macedo
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones molecular diameter.
Uses critical temperature and pressure. CSP method by [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma_{LJ}^3 = 0.17791 + 11.779 \left( \frac{T_c}{P_c}\right) - 0.049029\left( \frac{T_c}{P_c}\right)^2
$$

# Parameters

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

sigma

: Lennard-Jones molecular diameter, [Angstrom]

# Notes

Pc is originally in bar. An excellent paper. None is
returned if the polynomial returns a negative number, as in the case of
1029.13 K and 3.83 bar.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sigma_Silva_Liu_Macedo(560.1, 4550000)
5.164483998730177
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Silva, Carlos M., Hongqin Liu, and Eugenia A. Macedo. "Models for
    Self-Diffusion Coefficients of Dense Fluids, Including Hydrogen-Bonding
    Substances." Chemical Engineering Science 53, no. 13 (July 1, 1998):
    2423-29. doi:10.1016/S0009-2509(98)00037-2

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.collision_integral_Neufeld_Janzen_Aziz
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones collision integral for any of 16 values of
(l,j) for the wide range of 0.3 < T_star < 100. Values are accurate to
0.1 % of actual values, but the calculation of actual values is
computationally intensive and so these simplifications are used, developed
in [^footnote-1].

$$
\Omega_D = \frac{A}{T^{*B}} + \frac{C}{\exp(DT^*)} + \frac{E}{\exp(FT^{*})} + \frac{G}{\exp(HT^*)} + RT^{*B}\sin(ST^{*W}-P)
$$

# Parameters

Tstar

: Reduced temperature of the fluid [-]

l

: term

s

: term

# Returns

Omega

: Collision integral of A and B

# Notes

Acceptable pairs of (l,s) are (1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5),
(1, 6), (1, 7), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), (3, 3), (3, 4),
(3, 5), and (4, 4).

$$
T^* = \frac{k_b T}{\epsilon}
$$

Results are very similar to those of the more modern formulation,
`collision_integral_Kim_Monroe`.

Calculations begin to yield overflow errors in some values of (l, 2) after
T_star = 75, beginning with (1, 7). Also susceptible are (1, 5) and (1, 6).

# Examples

```pycon
>>> collision_integral_Neufeld_Janzen_Aziz(100, 1, 1)
0.516717697672334
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Neufeld, Philip D., A. R. Janzen, and R. A. Aziz. "Empirical
    Equations to Calculate 16 of the Transport Collision Integrals
    Omega(l, S)\* for the Lennard-Jones (12-6) Potential." The Journal of
    Chemical Physics 57, no. 3 (August 1, 1972): 1100-1102.
    doi:10.1063/1.1678363
## Collision Integral

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.collision_integral_Kim_Monroe
documentation:
Calculates Lennard-Jones collision integral for any of 16 values of
(l,j) for the wide range of 0.3 < T_star < 400. Values are accurate to
0.007 % of actual values, but the calculation of actual values is
computationally intensive and so these simplifications are used, developed
in [^footnote-1].

$$
\Omega^{(l,s)*} = A^{(l,s)} + \sum_{k=1}^6 \left[ \frac{B_k^{(l,s)}} {(T^*)^k} + C_k^{(l,s)} (\ln T^*)^k \right]
$$

# Parameters

Tstar

: Reduced temperature of the fluid [-]

l

: term

s

: term

# Returns

Omega

: Collision integral of A and B

# Notes

Acceptable pairs of (l,s) are (1, 1), (1, 2), (1, 3), (1, 4), (1, 5),
(1, 6), (1, 7), (2, 2), (2, 3), (2, 4), (2, 5), (2, 6), (3, 3), (3, 4),
(3, 5), and (4, 4).

$$
T^* = \frac{k_b T}{\epsilon}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> collision_integral_Kim_Monroe(400, 1, 1)
0.4141818082392228
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Kim, Sun Ung, and Charles W. Monroe. "High-Accuracy Calculations of
    Sixteen Collision Integrals for Lennard-Jones (12-6) Gases and Their
    Interpolation to Parameterize Neon, Argon, and Krypton." Journal of
    Computational Physics 273 (September 15, 2014): 358-73.
    doi:10.1016/j.jcp.2014.05.018.

functions.name: chemicals.lennard_jones.T_star
documentation:
This function calculates the parameter `T_star` as needed in performing
collision integral calculations.

$$
T^* = \frac{kT}{\epsilon}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> T_star(T=318.2, epsilon_k=308.43)
1.0316765554582887
```

# Parameters

epsilon_k

: Lennard-Jones depth of potential-energy minimum over k, [K]

epsilon

: Lennard-Jones depth of potential-energy minimum [J]

# Returns

T_star

: Dimentionless temperature for calculating collision integral, [-]

# Notes

Tabulated values are normally listed as epsilon/k. k is the Boltzman
constant, with units of J/K.

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bird, R. Byron, Warren E. Stewart, and Edwin N. Lightfoot.
    Transport Phenomena, Revised 2nd Edition. New York:
    John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 2006
## Misc

modules.name: chemicals.temperature
documentation:
Chemical Engineering Design Library (ChEDL). Utilities for process modeling.
Copyright (C) 2016, 2017, 2018, 2019 Caleb Bell \<<mailto:Caleb.Andrew.Bell@gmail.com>>

THE SOFTWARE IS PROVIDED "AS IS", WITHOUT WARRANTY OF ANY KIND, EXPRESS OR
IMPLIED, INCLUDING BUT NOT LIMITED TO THE WARRANTIES OF MERCHANTABILITY,
FITNESS FOR A PARTICULAR PURPOSE AND NONINFRINGEMENT. IN NO EVENT SHALL THE
AUTHORS OR COPYRIGHT HOLDERS BE LIABLE FOR ANY CLAIM, DAMAGES OR OTHER
LIABILITY, WHETHER IN AN ACTION OF CONTRACT, TORT OR OTHERWISE, ARISING FROM,
OUT OF OR IN CONNECTION WITH THE SOFTWARE OR THE USE OR OTHER DEALINGS IN THE
SOFTWARE.

This module contains functionality for converting between the temperature scales
ITS-90, ITS-76, ITS-68, ITS-48, and ITS-27. These historical temperature scales
can deviate quite a bit from modern temperature measurements! It is important
to convert old measurements of temperature to their modern equivalent.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```

# Conversion functions

```
.. autofunction:: chemicals.temperature.T_converter
```

```
.. autofunction:: chemicals.temperature.ITS90_68_difference
```

functions.name: chemicals.temperature.ITS90_68_difference
documentation:
Calculates the difference between ITS-90 and ITS-68 scales using a
series of models listed in [^footnote-1], [^footnote-2], and [^footnote-3].

The temperature difference is given by the following equations:

From 13.8 K to 73.15 K:

$$
T_{90} - T_{68} = a_0 + \sum_{i=1}^{12} a_i[(T_{90}/K-40)/40]^i
$$

From 83.8 K to 903.75 K:

$$
T_{90} - T_{68} = \sum_{i=1}^8 b_i[(T_{90}/K - 273.15)/630]^i
$$

From 903.75 K to 1337.33 K:

$$
T_{90} - T_{68} = \sum_{i=0}^5 c_i[T_{90}/^\circ C]^i
$$

Above 1337.33 K:

$$
T_{90} - T_{68} = -1.398\cdot 10^{-7}\left(\frac{T_{90}}{K}\right)^2
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, ITS-90, or approximately ITS-68 [K]

# Returns

dT

: Temperature, difference between ITS-90 and ITS-68 at T [K]

# Notes

The conversion is straightforward when T90 is known. Theoretically, the
model should be solved numerically to convert the reverse way. However,
according to [^footnote-4], the difference is under 0.05 mK from 73.15 K to
903.15 K, and under 0.26 mK up to 1337.33 K.

For temperatures under 13.8 K, no conversion is performed.

The first set of coefficients is:

-0.005903, 0.008174, -0.061924, -0.193388, 1.490793, 1.252347, -9.835868,
1.411912, 25.277595, -19.183815, -18.437089, 27.000895, -8.716324.

The second set of coefficients is:

0, -0.148759, -0.267408, 1.08076, 1.269056, -4.089591, -1.871251,
7.438081, -3.536296.

The third set of coefficients is:

7.8687209E1, -4.7135991E-1, 1.0954715E-3, -1.2357884E-6, 6.7736583E-10,
-1.4458081E-13.

These last coefficients use the temperature in degrees Celcius. A slightly
older model used the following coefficients but a different equation over
the same range:

-0.00317, -0.97737, 1.2559, 2.03295, -5.91887, -3.23561, 7.23364,
5.04151.

The model for these coefficients was:

$$
T_{90} - T_{68} = c_0 + \sum_{i=1}^7 c_i[(T_{90}/K - 1173.15)/300]^i
$$

For temperatures larger than several thousand K, the differences have no
meaning and grows quadratically.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> ITS90_68_difference(1000.)
0.01231818956580355
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bedford, R. E., G. Bonnier, H. Maas, and F. Pavese. "Techniques for
    Approximating the International Temperature Scale of 1990." Bureau
    International Des Poids et Mesures, Sfievres, 1990.

[^footnote-2]: Wier, Ron D., and Robert N. Goldberg. "On the Conversion of
    Thermodynamic Properties to the Basis of the International Temperature
    Scale of 1990." The Journal of Chemical Thermodynamics 28, no. 3
    (March 1996): 261-76. doi:10.1006/jcht.1996.0026.

[^footnote-3]: Goldberg, Robert N., and R. D. Weir. "Conversion of Temperatures
    and Thermodynamic Properties to the Basis of the International
    Temperature Scale of 1990 (Technical Report)." Pure and Applied
    Chemistry 64, no. 10 (1992): 1545-1562. doi:10.1351/pac199264101545.

[^footnote-4]: Code10.info. "Conversions among International Temperature Scales."
    Accessed May 22, 2016. <http://www.code10.info/index.php%3Foption%3Dcom_content%26view%3Darticle%26id%3D83:conversions-among-international-temperature-scales%26catid%3D60:temperature%26Itemid%3D83>.

functions.name: chemicals.temperature.T_converter
documentation:
Converts the a temperature reading made in any of the scales
'ITS-90', 'ITS-68','ITS-48', 'ITS-76', or 'ITS-27' to any of the other
scales. Not all temperature ranges can be converted to other ranges; for
instance, 'ITS-76' is purely for low temperatures, and 5 K on it has no
conversion to 'ITS-90' or any other scale. Both a conversion to ITS-90 and
to the desired scale must be possible for the conversion to occur.
The conversion uses cubic spline interpolation.

ITS-68 conversion is valid from 14 K to 4300 K.
ITS-48 conversion is valid from 93.15 K to 4273.15 K
ITS-76 conversion is valid from 5 K to 27 K.
ITS-27 is valid from 903.15 K to 4273.15 K.

# Parameters

T

: Temperature, on `current` scale [K]

current

: String representing the scale T is in, 'ITS-90', 'ITS-68',
  'ITS-48', 'ITS-76', or 'ITS-27'.

desired

: String representing the scale T will be returned in, 'ITS-90',
  'ITS-68', 'ITS-48', 'ITS-76', or 'ITS-27'.

# Returns

T

: Temperature, on scale `desired` [K]

# Notes

Because the conversion is performed by spline functions, a re-conversion
of a value will not yield exactly the original value. However, it is quite
close.

The use of splines is quite quick (20 micro seconds/calculation). While
just a spline for one-way conversion could be used, a numerical solver
would have to be used to obtain an exact result for the reverse conversion.
This was found to take approximately 1 ms/calculation, depending on the
region.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> T_converter(500, 'ITS-68', 'ITS-48')
499.9470
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Wier, Ron D., and Robert N. Goldberg. "On the Conversion of
    Thermodynamic Properties to the Basis of the International Temperature
    Scale of 1990." The Journal of Chemical Thermodynamics 28, no. 3
    (March 1996): 261-76. doi:10.1006/jcht.1996.0026.

[^footnote-2]: Goldberg, Robert N., and R. D. Weir. "Conversion of Temperatures
    and Thermodynamic Properties to the Basis of the International
    Temperature Scale of 1990 (Technical Report)." Pure and Applied
    Chemistry 64, no. 10 (1992): 1545-1562. doi:10.1351/pac199264101545.

routines, and sublimation pressure routines.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
