functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.PPDS8
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of a liquid using the 4-term
`tau` polynomial developed by the PPDS and named PPDS equation 8.

$$
k_l = a_0\left(1 + \sum_i^3 a_i\tau^{i/3} \right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

a0

: Coefficient, [-]

a1

: Coefficient, [-]

a2

: Coefficient, [-]

a3

: Coefficient, [-]

# Returns

k

: Low pressure liquid thermal conductivity, [W/(m\*K)]

# Notes

# Examples

Sample coefficients for benzene in [^footnote-1], at 500 K:

```pycon
>>> PPDS8(T=500.0, Tc=562.05, a0=0.0641126, a1=0.61057, a2=-1.72442, a3=3.94394)
0.08536381765218425
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "ThermoData Engine (TDE103b V10.1) User\`s Guide."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/Help/TDE103b/Eqns-Pure-ThermalCondSatL/PPDS8.htm>

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.PPDS3
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of a low-pressure gas using the 3-term
`Tr` polynomial developed by the PPDS and named PPDS equation 3.

$$
k_g = \sqrt{T_r}\left( \sum_{i=1}^3 \frac{a_i}{T_r^i} \right)^{-1}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

a1

: Coefficient, [-]

a2

: Coefficient, [-]

a3

: Coefficient, [-]

# Returns

k

: Low pressure gas thermal conductivity, [W/(m\*K)]

# Notes

# Examples

Sample coefficients for pentane in [^footnote-1], at 400 K:

```pycon
>>> PPDS3(T=400.0, Tc=470.008, a1=11.6366, a2=25.1191, a3=-7.21674)
0.0251734811601927
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "ThermoData Engine (TDE103b V10.1) User\`s Guide."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/Help/TDE103b/Eqns-Pure-ThermalCondG/PPDS3-ThermCondGas.htm>

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Chemsep_16
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of a low-pressure liquid using the
5-term `T` exponential polynomial found in ChemSep.

$$
k_l = A + \exp\left(\frac{B}{T} + C + DT + ET^2 \right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

A

: Coefficient, [W/(m\*K)]

B

: Coefficient, [K]

C

: Coefficient, [-]

D

: Coefficient, [1/K]

E

: Coefficient, [1/K^2]

# Returns

k

: Low pressure liquid thermal conductivity, [W/(m\*K)]

# Notes

This correlation is also used by ChemSep for surface tension. Calculation of
negative values is prevented and those are set to 0.

# Examples

Sample coefficients for liquid thermal conductivity of n-hexane in [^footnote-1], at
300 K:

```pycon
>>> Chemsep_16(300.0, -0.12682, -1.5015, -1.0467, -0.00088709, -9.3679E-07)
0.11924904787869
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Kooijman, Harry A., and Ross Taylor. The ChemSep Book. Books on
    Demand Norderstedt, Germany, 2000.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.k_IAPWS
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of water or steam according to the
2011 IAPWS [^footnote-1] formulation. Critical enhancement is ignored unless
parameters for it are provided.

$$
\bar\lambda = \bar\lambda_0\times \bar\lambda_1(\bar T, \bar \rho) + \bar\lambda_2(\bar T, \bar\rho)
$$

$$
\bar\lambda_0 = \frac{\sqrt{\bar T}} {\sum_{k=0}^4 \frac{L_k}{\bar T^k}}
$$

$$
\bar \lambda_1(\bar T, \bar \rho) = \exp\left[ \bar\rho \sum_{i=0}^4 \left(\left(\frac{1}{\bar T} - 1 \right)^i \sum_{j=0}^5 L_{ij}(\bar\rho - 1)^j\right)\right]
$$

$$
\bar\lambda_2 = \Gamma\frac{\bar\rho \bar c_p \bar T}{\bar \mu} Z(y)
$$

$$
Z(y) = \frac{2}{\pi y} \left\{\left[(1 - \kappa^{-1})\arctan(y) + \kappa^{-1}y\right] - \left[1 - \exp\left(\frac{-1}{y^{-1} + y^{-2}/3\bar\rho^2}\right)\right]\right\}
$$

$$
y = \bar q_D \xi(\bar T, \bar \rho)
$$

$$
\xi = \xi_0 \left(\frac{\Delta \bar\chi}{\Gamma_0}\right)^{\nu/\gamma}
$$

$$
\Delta \bar\chi(\bar T, \bar \rho) = \bar\rho\left[ \zeta(\bar T, \bar \rho) - \zeta(\bar T_R, \bar \rho)\frac{\bar T_R}{\bar T} \right]
$$

$$
\zeta = \left(\frac{\partial \bar \rho}{\partial \bar p}\right)_{\bar T}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature water [K]

rho

: Density of water [kg/m^3]

Cp

: Constant pressure heat capacity of water, [J/kg/K]

Cv

: Constant volume heat capacity of water, [J/kg/K]

mu

: Viscosity of water, [Pa\*s]

drho_dP

: Partial derivative of density with respect to pressure at constant
  temperature, [kg/m^3/Pa]

drho_dP_Tr

: Partial derivative of density with respect to pressure at constant
  temperature (at the reference temperature (970.644 K) and the actual
  density of water); will be calculated from the industrial formulation
  fit if omitted, [kg/m^3/Pa]

# Returns

k

: Thermal condiuctivity, [W/m/K]

# Notes

Gamma = 177.8514;

qd = 0.4E-9;

nu = 0.630;

gamma = 1.239;

zeta0 = 0.13E-9;

Gamma0 = 0.06;

TRC = 1.5

The formulation uses the industrial variant of the critical enhancement.
It matches to 5E-6 relative tolerance at the check temperature, and should
match even closer outside it.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> k_IAPWS(647.35, 750.)
0.5976194153179502
```

Region 1, test 1, from MPEI, exact match:

```pycon
>>> k_IAPWS(T=620., rho=613.227777440324, Cp=7634.337046792,
... Cv=3037.934412104, mu=70.905106751524E-6, drho_dP=5.209378197916E-6)
0.48148519510200044
```

Full scientific calculation:

```pycon
>>> from chemicals.iapws import iapws95_properties, iapws95_P, iapws95_Tc
>>> from chemicals.viscosity import mu_IAPWS
>>> T, P = 298.15, 1e5
>>> rho, _, _, _, Cv, Cp, _, _, _, _, drho_dP = iapws95_properties(T, P)
>>> P_ref = iapws95_P(1.5*iapws95_Tc, rho)
>>> _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, _, drho_dP_Tr = iapws95_properties(1.5*iapws95_Tc, P_ref)
>>> mu = mu_IAPWS(T, rho, drho_dP, drho_dP_Tr)
>>> k_IAPWS(T, rho, Cp, Cv, mu, drho_dP, drho_dP_Tr)
0.60651532815
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Huber, M. L., R. A. Perkins, D. G. Friend, J. V. Sengers, M. J.
    Assael, I. N. Metaxa, K. Miyagawa, R. Hellmann, and E. Vogel. "New
    International Formulation for the Thermal Conductivity of H2O."
    Journal of Physical and Chemical Reference Data 41, no. 3 (September 1,
    2012): 033102. doi:10.1063/1.4738955.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.k_air_lemmon
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of air using the Lemmon and Jacobsen
(2004) [^footnote-1] formulation. The critical enhancement term is ignored unless
all the rquired parameters for it are provided.

$$
\lambda = \lambda^0(T) + \lambda^r(\tau, \delta) + \lambda^c(\tau, \delta)
$$

$$
\lambda^0 = N_1\left[\frac{\eta^0(T)}{1 \mu \text{Pa}\cdot \text{s}} \right] + N_2\tau^{t_2} + N_3\tau^{t_3}
$$

$$
\lambda^r = \sum_{i=4}^n N_i \tau^{t_i} \delta^{d_i} \exp(-\gamma_i \delta^{l_i})
$$

$$
\lambda^c = \rho C_p \frac{kR_0 T}{6\pi\xi\cdot \eta(T, \rho)}\left( \tilde \Omega -\tilde \Omega_0\right)
$$

$$
\tilde \Omega = \frac{2}{\pi}\left[ \left(\frac{C_p - C_v}{C_p}\right)\tan^{-1} (\xi/q_D) + \frac{C_v} {C_p}(\xi/q_D) \right]
$$

$$
\tilde \Omega_0 = \frac{2}{\pi}\left\{1 - \exp\left[\frac{-1}{q_D/\xi + 1/3(\xi/q_D)^2(\rho_c/\rho)^2} \right] \right\}
$$

$$
\xi = \xi_0 \left[\frac{\tilde \chi(T, \rho) - \tilde \chi(T_{ref}, \rho)\frac{T_{ref}}{T}}{\Gamma}  \right]^{\nu/\gamma}
$$

$$
\tilde \chi(T, \rho) = \frac{P_c \rho}{\rho_c^2} \left(\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial P} \right)_{T}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature air [K]

rho

: Molar density of air [mol/m^3]

Cp

: Molar constant pressure heat capacity of air, [J/mol/K]

Cv

: Molar constant volume heat capacity of air, [J/mol/K]

mu

: Viscosity of air, [Pa\*s]

drho_dP

: Partial derivative of density with respect to pressure at constant
  temperature, [mol/m^3/Pa]

drho_dP_Tr

: Partial derivative of density with respect to pressure at constant
  temperature (at the reference temperature (265.262 K) and the actual
  density of air), [mol/m^3/Pa]

# Returns

k

: Thermal condiuctivity of air, [W/m/K]

# Notes

The constnts are as follows:

Ni = [1.308, 1.405, -1.036, 8.743, 14.76, -16.62, 3.793, -6.142, -0.3778]

ti = [None, -1.1, -0.3, 0.1, 0.0, 0.5, 2.7, 0.3, 1.3]

di = [None, None, None, 1, 2, 3, 7, 7, 11]

li = [None, None, None, 0, 0, 2, 2, 2, 2]

gammai = [None, None, None, 0, 0, 1, 1, 1, 1]

R0 = 1.01; Pc = 3.78502E6 Pa; xi0 = 0.11E-9 nm; qd = 0.31E-9 nm;
Tc = 132.6312 K (actually the maxcondentherm); T_ref = 265.262 (2Tc
rounded differently); rhoc = 10447.7 mol/m^3 (actually the maxcondentherm);
k = 1.380658E-23 J/K; nu = 0.63 and gamma = 1.2415, sigma = 0.36,
MW = 28.9586 g/mol.

# Examples

Basic calculation at 300 K and approximately 1 bar:

```pycon
>>> k_air_lemmon(300, 40.0)
0.0263839695044
```

Calculation near critical point:

```pycon
>>> k_air_lemmon(132.64, 10400, 2137.078854678728, 35.24316159996235, 0.07417878614315769, 0.00035919027241528256, 1.7762253265868595e-05)
0.07562307234760
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Lemmon, E. W., and R. T. Jacobsen. "Viscosity and Thermal
    Conductivity Equations for Nitrogen, Oxygen, Argon, and Air."
    International Journal of Thermophysics 25, no. 1 (January 1, 2004):
    21-69. <https://doi.org/10.1023/B:IJOT.0000022327.04529.f3>.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Sheffy_Johnson
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of a liquid as a function of
temperature using the Sheffy-Johnson (1961) method. Requires
Temperature, molecular weight, and melting point.

$$
k = 1.951 \frac{1-0.00126(T-T_m)}{T_m^{0.216}MW^{0.3}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tm

: Melting point of the fluid [K]

# Returns

kl

: Thermal conductivity of the fluid, W/m/k

# Notes

The origin of this equation has been challenging to trace. It is
presently unknown, and untested.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Sheffy_Johnson(300, 47, 280)
0.17740150413112193
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Scheffy, W. J., and E. F. Johnson. "Thermal Conductivities of
    Liquids at High Temperatures." Journal of Chemical & Engineering Data
    6, no. 2 (April 1, 1961): 245-49. doi:10.1021/je60010a019
## Purely CSP Methods - Liquids

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Sato_Riedel
documentation:
Calculate the thermal conductivity of a liquid as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Sato-Riedel [^footnote-1], [^footnote-2], published in
Reid [^footnote-3]. Requires temperature, molecular weight, and boiling and critical
temperatures.

$$
k = \frac{1.1053}{\sqrt{MW}}\frac{3+20(1-T_r)^{2/3}} {3+20(1-T_{br})^{2/3}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

This equation has a complicated history. It is proposed by Reid [^footnote-3].
Limited accuracy should be expected. Uncheecked.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Sato_Riedel(300, 47, 390, 520)
0.21037692461337687
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Riedel, L.: Chem. Ing. Tech., 21, 349 (1949); 23: 59, 321, 465 (1951)

[^footnote-2]: Maejima, T., private communication, 1973

[^footnote-3]: Properties of Gases and Liquids", 3rd Ed., McGraw-Hill, 1977

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Lakshmi_Prasad
documentation:
Estimates thermal conductivity of pure liquids as a function of
temperature using a reference fluid approach. Low accuracy but quick.
Developed using several organic fluids.

$$
\lambda = 0.0655-0.0005T + \frac{1.3855-0.00197T}{MW^{0.5}}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

This equation returns negative numbers at high T sometimes.
This equation is one of those implemented by DDBST.
If this results in a negative thermal conductivity, no value is returned.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Lakshmi_Prasad(273.15, 100)
0.013664450
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Lakshmi, D. S., and D. H. L. Prasad. "A Rapid Estimation Method for
    Thermal Conductivity of Pure Liquids." The Chemical Engineering Journal
    48, no. 3 (April 1992): 211-14. doi:10.1016/0300-9467(92)80037-B

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Gharagheizi_liquid
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a liquid as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Gharagheizi [^footnote-1]. A convoluted
method claiming high-accuracy and using only statistically significant
variable following analalysis.

Requires temperature, molecular weight, boiling temperature and critical
pressure and acentric factor.

$$
k = 10^{-4}\left[10\omega + 2P_c-2T+4+1.908(T_b+\frac{1.009B^2}{MW^2}) +\frac{3.9287MW^4}{B^4}+\frac{A}{B^8}\right]
$$

$$
A = 3.8588MW^8(1.0045B+6.5152MW-8.9756)
$$

$$
B = 16.0407MW+2T_b-27.9074
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor of the fluid [-]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Pressure is internally converted into bar, as used in the original equation.

This equation was derived with 19000 points representing 1640 unique compounds.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Gharagheizi_liquid(300, 40, 350, 1E6, 0.27)
0.2171113029534838
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gharagheizi, Farhad, Poorandokht Ilani-Kashkouli, Mehdi Sattari,
    Amir H. Mohammadi, Deresh Ramjugernath, and Dominique Richon.
    "Development of a General Model for Determination of Thermal
    Conductivity of Liquid Chemical Compounds at Atmospheric Pressure."
    AIChE Journal 59, no. 5 (May 1, 2013): 1702-8. doi:10.1002/aic.13938

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Nicola_original
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a liquid as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Nicola [^footnote-1]. A simpler but long
method claiming high-accuracy and using only statistically significant
variable following analalysis.

Requires temperature, molecular weight, critical temperature, acentric
factor and the heat of vaporization.

$$
\frac{\lambda}{1 \text{Wm/K}}=-0.5694-0.1436T_r+5.4893\times10^{-10} \frac{\Delta_{fus}H}{\text{kmol/J}}+0.0508\omega + \left(\frac{1 \text{kg/kmol}}{MW}\right)^{0.0622}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

omega

: Acentric factor of the fluid [-]

Hfus

: Heat of fusion of the fluid [J/mol]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

A weird statistical correlation. Recent and yet to be reviewed.
This correlation has been superceded by the author's later work.
Hfus is internally converted to be in J/kmol.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Nicola_original(300, 142.3, 611.7, 0.49, 201853)
0.2305018632230984
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Nicola, Giovanni Di, Eleonora Ciarrocchi, Mariano Pierantozzi, and
    Roman Stryjek. "A New Equation for the Thermal Conductivity of Organic
    Compounds." Journal of Thermal Analysis and Calorimetry 116, no. 1
    (April 1, 2014): 135-40. doi:10.1007/s10973-013-3422-7

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Nicola
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a liquid as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of [^footnote-1]. A statistically derived
equation using any correlated terms.

Requires temperature, molecular weight, critical temperature and pressure,
and acentric factor.

$$
\frac{\lambda}{0.5147 W/m/K} = -0.2537T_r+\frac{0.0017Pc}{\text{bar}} +0.1501 \omega + \left(\frac{1}{MW}\right)^{-0.2999}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor of the fluid [-]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

A statistical correlation. A revision of an original correlation.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Nicola(300, 142.3, 611.7, 2110000.0, 0.49)
0.10863821554584034
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Di Nicola, Giovanni, Eleonora Ciarrocchi, Gianluca Coccia, and
    Mariano Pierantozzi. "Correlations of Thermal Conductivity for
    Liquid Refrigerants at Atmospheric Pressure or near Saturation."
    International Journal of Refrigeration. 2014.
    doi:10.1016/j.ijrefrig.2014.06.003

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Bahadori_liquid
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of parafin liquid hydrocarbons.
Fits their data well, and is useful as only MW is required.
X is the Molecular weight, and Y the temperature.

$$
K = a + bY + CY^2 + dY^3
$$

$$
a = A_1 + B_1 X + C_1 X^2 + D_1 X^3
$$

$$
b = A_2 + B_2 X + C_2 X^2 + D_2 X^3
$$

$$
c = A_3 + B_3 X + C_3 X^2 + D_3 X^3
$$

$$
d = A_4 + B_4 X + C_4 X^2 + D_4 X^3
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

The accuracy of this equation has not been reviewed.

# Examples

Data point from [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> Bahadori_liquid(273.15, 170)
0.1427427810827268
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bahadori, Alireza, and Saeid Mokhatab. "Estimating Thermal
    Conductivity of Hydrocarbons." Chemical Engineering 115, no. 13
    (December 2008): 52-54

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.kl_Mersmann_Kind
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of organic liquid substances
according to the method of [^footnote-1].

$$
\lambda^* = \frac{\lambda\cdot V_c^{2/3}\cdot T_c\cdot \text{MW}^{0.5}} {(k\cdot T_c)^{1.5}\cdot N_A^{7/6}}
$$

$$
\lambda^* = \frac{2}{3}\left(n_a + 40\sqrt{1-T_r}\right)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

Vc

: Critical volume of the fluid [m^3/mol]

na

: Number of atoms in the molecule, [-]

# Returns

kl

: Estimated liquid thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

In the equation, all quantities must be in SI units but N_A is in a kmol
basis and Vc is in units of (m^3/kmol); this is converted internally.

# Examples

Dodecane at 400 K:

```pycon
>>> kl_Mersmann_Kind(400, 170.33484, 658.0,
... 0.000754, 38)
0.0895271829899285
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mersmann, Alfons, and Matthias Kind. "Prediction of Mechanical and
    Thermal Properties of Pure Liquids, of Critical Data, and of Vapor
    Pressure." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, January 31,
    2017\. <https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.iecr.6b04323>.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.DIPPR9G
documentation:
Adjustes for pressure the thermal conductivity of a liquid using an
emperical formula based on [^footnote-1], but as given in [^footnote-2].

$$
k = k^* \left[ 0.98 + 0.0079 P_r T_r^{1.4} + 0.63 T_r^{1.2} \left( \frac{P_r}{30 + P_r}\right)\right]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

P

: Pressure of fluid [Pa]

Tc: float

: Critical point of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

kl

: Thermal conductivity of liquid at 1 atm or saturation, [W/m/K]

# Returns

kl_dense

: Thermal conductivity of liquid at P, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entrely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
The original source has not been reviewed.

This is DIPPR Procedure 9G: Method for the Thermal Conductivity of Pure
Nonhydrocarbon Liquids at High Pressures

# Examples

From [^footnote-2], for butyl acetate.

```pycon
>>> DIPPR9G(515.05, 3.92E7, 579.15, 3.212E6, 7.085E-2)
0.0864419738671184
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Missenard, F. A., Thermal Conductivity of Organic Liquids of a
    Series or a Group of Liquids , Rev. Gen.Thermodyn., 101 649 (1970).

[^footnote-2]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.
## Thermal Conductivity of Dense Liquids

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Missenard
documentation:
Adjustes for pressure the thermal conductivity of a liquid using an
emperical formula based on [^footnote-1], but as given in [^footnote-2].

$$
\frac{k}{k^*} = 1 + Q P_r^{0.7}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

P

: Pressure of fluid [Pa]

Tc: float

: Critical point of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

kl

: Thermal conductivity of liquid at 1 atm or saturation, [W/m/K]

# Returns

kl_dense

: Thermal conductivity of liquid at P, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
An interpolation routine is used here from tabulated values of Q.
The original source has not been reviewed.

# Examples

Example from [^footnote-2], toluene; matches.

```pycon
>>> Missenard(304., 6330E5, 591.8, 41E5, 0.129)
0.2198375777069657
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Missenard, F. A., Thermal Conductivity of Organic Liquids of a
    Series or a Group of Liquids , Rev. Gen.Thermodyn., 101 649 (1970).

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.DIPPR9H
documentation:
Calculates thermal conductivity of a liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1] and also in [^footnote-2].

$$
\lambda_m = \left( \sum_i w_i \lambda_i^{-2}\right)^{-1/2}
$$

This is also called the Vredeveld (1973) equation. A review in [^footnote-3] finds
this the best model on average. However, they did caution that in some
cases a linear mole-fraction mixing rule performs better. This equation
according to Poling [^footnote-1] should not be used if some components have
thermal conductivities more than twice other components. They also say this
should not be used with water.

# Parameters

ws

: Mass fractions of components

ks

: Liquid thermal conductivites of all components, [W/m/K]

# Returns

kl

: Thermal conductivity of liquid mixture, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
The example is from [^footnote-2]; all results agree.
The original source has not been reviewed.

DIPPR Procedure 9H: Method for the Thermal Conductivity of Nonaqueous Liquid Mixtures

Average deviations of 3%. for 118 nonaqueous systems with 817 data points.
Max deviation 20%. According to DIPPR.

In some sources, this equation is given with the molecular weights included:

$$
\lambda_m^{-2} = \frac{\sum_i z_i {MW}_i \lambda_i^{-2}} {\sum_i z_i {MW}_i}
$$

# Examples

```pycon
>>> DIPPR9H([0.258, 0.742], [0.1692, 0.1528])
0.15657104706719646
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E. The
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

[^footnote-2]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.

[^footnote-3]: Focke, Walter W. "Correlating Thermal-Conductivity Data for Ternary
    Liquid Mixtures." International Journal of Thermophysics 29, no. 4
    (August 1, 2008): 1342-60. <https://doi.org/10.1007/s10765-008-0465-2>.
## Thermal conductivity of liquid mixtures

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.DIPPR9I
documentation:
Calculates thermal conductivity of a liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1]. This is recommended in [^footnote-2] for aqueous and
nonaqueous systems.

$$
k_{mix} = \sum_{i}\sum_j \phi_i\phi_j k_{i,j}
$$

$$
k_{i,j} = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{k_i} + \frac{1}{k_j}}
$$

$$
\phi_i = \frac{z_i V_{m,i}}{\sum_j^n z_j V_{m,j}}
$$

# Parameters

zs

: Mole fractions of components, [-]

Vms

: Molar volumes of each component, [m^3/mol]

ks

: Liquid thermal conductivites of all components, [W/m/K]

# Returns

kl

: Thermal conductivity of liquid mixture, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
The example is from [^footnote-2]; all results agree.

[^footnote-2] found average deviations of 4-6% for 118 nonaqueous systems
and 15 aqueous systems at atmospheric pressure, with a maximum deviation of
33%.

The computational complexity here is N^2, with a division present in the
inner loop.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> DIPPR9I(zs=[.682, .318], Vms=[1.723e-2, 7.338e-2], ks=[.6037, .1628])
0.25397430656658937
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Li, C. C. "Thermal Conductivity of Liquid Mixtures." AIChE Journal
    22, no. 5 (1976): 927-30. <https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.690220520>.

[^footnote-2]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Filippov
documentation:
Calculates thermal conductivity of a binary liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-2] as found in [^footnote-1].

$$
\lambda_m = w_1 \lambda_1 + w_2\lambda_2 - 0.72 w_1 w_2(\lambda_2-\lambda_1)
$$

# Parameters

ws

: Mass fractions of components

ks

: Liquid thermal conductivites of all components, [W/m/K]

# Returns

kl

: Thermal conductivity of liquid mixture, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
The original source has not been reviewed.
Only useful for binary mixtures.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Filippov([0.258, 0.742], [0.1692, 0.1528])
0.15929167628799998
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E. The
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

[^footnote-2]: Filippov, L. P.: Vest. Mosk. Univ., Ser. Fiz. Mat. Estestv. Nauk,
    (8I0E): 67-69A955); Chem. Abstr., 50: 8276 A956).
    Filippov, L. P., and N. S. Novoselova: Vestn. Mosk. Univ., Ser. F
    iz. Mat. Estestv.Nauk, CI0B): 37-40A955); Chem. Abstr., 49: 11366 A955).

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Eucken
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Eucken [^footnote-1].

$$
\frac{\lambda MW}{\eta C_v} = 1 + \frac{9/4}{C_v/R}
$$

# Parameters

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

mu

: Gas viscosity [Pa\*s]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Temperature dependence is introduced via heat capacity and viscosity.
A theoretical equation. No original author located.
MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.

# Examples

2-methylbutane at low pressure, 373.15 K. Mathes calculation in [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> Eucken(MW=72.151, Cvm=135.9, mu=8.77E-6)
0.018792645058456698
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.
## Thermal Conductivity of Gases

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Eucken_modified
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas as a function of
temperature using the Modified CSP method of Eucken [^footnote-1].

$$
\frac{\lambda MW}{\eta C_v} = 1.32 + \frac{1.77}{C_v/R}
$$

# Parameters

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

mu

: Gas viscosity [Pa\*s]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Temperature dependence is introduced via heat capacity and viscosity.
A theoretical equation. No original author located.
MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.

# Examples

2-methylbutane at low pressure, 373.15 K. Mathes calculation in [^footnote-1].

```pycon
>>> Eucken_modified(MW=72.151, Cvm=135.9, mu=8.77E-6)
0.02359353760551249
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.DIPPR9B
documentation:
Calculates the thermal conductivity of a gas using one of several
emperical equations developed in [^footnote-1], [^footnote-2], and presented in [^footnote-3].

For monoatomic gases:

$$
k = 2.5 \frac{\eta C_v}{MW}
$$

For linear molecules:

$$
k = \frac{\eta}{MW} \left( 1.30 C_v + 14644.00 - \frac{2928.80}{T_r}\right)
$$

For nonlinear molecules:

$$
k = \frac{\eta}{MW}(1.15C_v + 16903.36)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

MW

: Molwcular weight of fluid [g/mol]

Cvm

: Molar heat capacity at constant volume of fluid, [J/mol/K]

mu

: Viscosity of gas, [Pa\*s]

# Returns

k_g

: Thermal conductivity of gas, [W/m/k]

# Notes

Tested with DIPPR values.
Cvm is internally converted to J/kmol/K.

# Examples

CO:

```pycon
>>> DIPPR9B(200., 28.01, 20.826, 1.277E-5, 132.92, chemtype='linear')
0.01813208676438415
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bromley, LeRoy A., Berkeley. University of California, and U.S.
    Atomic Energy Commission. Thermal Conductivity of Gases at Moderate
    Pressures. UCRL;1852. Berkeley, CA: University of California Radiation
    Laboratory, 1952.

[^footnote-2]: Stiel, Leonard I., and George Thodos. "The Thermal Conductivity of
    Nonpolar Substances in the Dense Gaseous and Liquid Regions." AIChE
    Journal 10, no. 1 (January 1, 1964): 26-30. doi:10.1002/aic.690100114

[^footnote-3]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Chung
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Chung [^footnote-1].

$$
\frac{\lambda MW}{\eta C_v} = \frac{3.75 \Psi}{C_v/R}
$$

$$
\Psi = 1 + \alpha \left\{[0.215+0.28288\alpha-1.061\beta+0.26665Z]/ [0.6366+\beta Z + 1.061 \alpha \beta]\right\}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{C_v}{R}-1.5
$$

$$
\beta = 0.7862-0.7109\omega + 1.3168\omega^2
$$

$$
Z=2+10.5T_r^2
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the gas [K]

omega

: Acentric factor of the gas [-]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

mu

: Gas viscosity [Pa\*s]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.

# Examples

2-methylbutane at low pressure, 373.15 K. Mathes calculation in [^footnote-2].

```pycon
>>> Chung(T=373.15, MW=72.151, Tc=460.4, omega=0.227, Cvm=135.9, mu=8.77E-6)
0.023015653797111124
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chung, Ting Horng, Lloyd L. Lee, and Kenneth E. Starling.
    "Applications of Kinetic Gas Theories and Multiparameter Correlation for
    Prediction of Dilute Gas Viscosity and Thermal Conductivity."
    Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 23, no. 1
    (February 1, 1984): 8-13. doi:10.1021/i100013a002

[^footnote-2]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Eli_Hanley
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas as a function of
temperature using the reference fluid method of Eli and Hanley [^footnote-1] as
shown in [^footnote-2].

$$
\lambda = \lambda^* + \frac{\eta^*}{MW}(1.32)\left(C_v - \frac{3R}{2}\right)
$$

$$
Tr = \text{min}(Tr, 2)
$$

$$
\theta = 1 + (\omega-0.011)\left(0.56553 - 0.86276\ln Tr - \frac{0.69852}{Tr}\right)
$$

$$
\psi = [1 + (\omega - 0.011)(0.38560 - 1.1617\ln Tr)]\frac{0.288}{Z_c}
$$

$$
f = \frac{T_c}{190.4}\theta
$$

$$
h = \frac{V_c}{9.92E-5}\psi
$$

$$
T_0 = T/f
$$

$$
\eta_0^*(T_0)= \sum_{n=1}^9 C_n T_0^{(n-4)/3}
$$

$$
\theta_0 = 1944 \eta_0
$$

$$
\lambda^* = \lambda_0 H
$$

$$
\eta^* = \eta^*_0 H \frac{MW}{16.04}
$$

$$
H = \left(\frac{16.04}{MW}\right)^{0.5}f^{0.5}/h^{2/3}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the gas [K]

Vc

: Critical volume of the gas [m^3/mol]

Zc

: Critical compressibility of the gas []

omega

: Acentric factor of the gas [-]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Reference fluid is Methane.
MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.

# Examples

2-methylbutane at low pressure, 373.15 K. Matches calculation in [^footnote-2].

```pycon
>>> Eli_Hanley(T=373.15, MW=72.151, Tc=460.4, Vc=3.06E-4, Zc=0.267,
... omega=0.227, Cvm=135.9)
0.02247951724513664
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Ely, James F., and H. J. M. Hanley. "Prediction of Transport
    Properties. 2. Thermal Conductivity of Pure Fluids and Mixtures."
    Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 22, no. 1 (February 1,
    1983): 90-97. doi:10.1021/i100009a016.

[^footnote-2]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Gharagheizi_gas
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas as a function of
temperature using the CSP method of Gharagheizi [^footnote-1]. A convoluted
method claiming high-accuracy and using only statistically significant
variable following analalysis.

Requires temperature, molecular weight, boiling temperature and critical
pressure and acentric factor.

$$
k = 7.9505\times 10^{-4} + 3.989\times 10^{-5} T -5.419\times 10^-5 MW + 3.989\times 10^{-5} A
$$

$$
A = \frac{\left(2\omega + T - \frac{(2\omega + 3.2825)T}{T_b} + 3.2825\right)}{0.1MP_cT}  \times (3.9752\omega + 0.1 P_c + 1.9876B + 6.5243)^2
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

MW: float

: Molecular weight of the fluid [g/mol]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor of the fluid [-]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Pressure is internally converted into 10\*kPa but author used correlation with
kPa; overall, errors have been corrected in the presentation of the formula.

This equation was derived with 15927 points and 1574 compounds.
Example value from [^footnote-1] is the first point in the supportinf info, for CH4.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Gharagheizi_gas(580., 16.04246, 111.66, 4599000.0, 0.0115478000)
0.09594861261873211
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gharagheizi, Farhad, Poorandokht Ilani-Kashkouli, Mehdi Sattari,
    Amir H. Mohammadi, Deresh Ramjugernath, and Dominique Richon.
    "Development of a General Model for Determination of Thermal
    Conductivity of Liquid Chemical Compounds at Atmospheric Pressure."
    AIChE Journal 59, no. 5 (May 1, 2013): 1702-8. doi:10.1002/aic.13938

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Bahadori_gas
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of hydrocarbons gases at low P.
Fits their data well, and is useful as only MW is required.
Y is the Molecular weight, and X the temperature.

$$
K = a + bY + CY^2 + dY^3
$$

$$
a = A_1 + B_1 X + C_1 X^2 + D_1 X^3
$$

$$
b = A_2 + B_2 X + C_2 X^2 + D_2 X^3
$$

$$
c = A_3 + B_3 X + C_3 X^2 + D_3 X^3
$$

$$
d = A_4 + B_4 X + C_4 X^2 + D_4 X^3
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

The accuracy of this equation has not been reviewed.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Bahadori_gas(40+273.15, 20.0) # Point from article
0.03196816533787329
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Bahadori, Alireza, and Saeid Mokhatab. "Estimating Thermal
    Conductivity of Hydrocarbons." Chemical Engineering 115, no. 13
    (December 2008): 52-54

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Stiel_Thodos_dense
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas at high pressure as a
function of temperature using difference method of Stiel and Thodos [^footnote-1]
as shown in [^footnote-2].

if $\rho_r < 0.5$:

$$
(\lambda-\lambda^\circ)\Gamma Z_c^5=1.22\times 10^{-2} [\exp(0.535 \rho_r)-1]
$$

if $0.5 < \rho_r < 2.0$:

$$
(\lambda-\lambda^\circ)\Gamma Z_c^5=1.22\times 10^{-2} [\exp(0.535 \rho_r)-1]
$$

if $2 < \rho_r < 2.8$:

$$
(\lambda-\lambda^\circ)\Gamma Z_c^5=1.22\times 10^{-2} [\exp(0.535 \rho_r)-1]
$$

$$
\Gamma = 210 \left(\frac{T_cMW^3}{P_c^4}\right)^{1/6}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the gas [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the gas [Pa]

Vc

: Critical volume of the gas [m^3/mol]

Zc

: Critical compressibility of the gas [-]

Vm

: Molar volume of the gas at T and P [m^3/mol]

kg

: Low-pressure gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated dense gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Pc is internally converted to bar.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Stiel_Thodos_dense(T=378.15, MW=44.013, Tc=309.6, Pc=72.4E5,
... Vc=97.4E-6, Zc=0.274, Vm=144E-6, kg=2.34E-2)
0.041245574404863684
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Stiel, Leonard I., and George Thodos. "The Thermal Conductivity of
    Nonpolar Substances in the Dense Gaseous and Liquid Regions." AIChE
    Journal 10, no. 1 (January 1, 1964): 26-30. doi:10.1002/aic.690100114.

[^footnote-2]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.
## Thermal Conductivity of dense gases

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Eli_Hanley_dense
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas at high pressure as a
function of temperature using the reference fluid method of Eli and
Hanley [^footnote-1] as shown in [^footnote-2].

$$
Tr = min(Tr, 2)
$$

$$
Vr = min(Vr, 2)
$$

$$
f = \frac{T_c}{190.4}\theta
$$

$$
h = \frac{V_c}{9.92E-5}\psi
$$

$$
T_0 = T/f
$$

$$
\rho_0 = \frac{16.04}{V}h
$$

$$
\theta = 1 + (\omega-0.011)\left(0.09057 - 0.86276\ln Tr + \left( 0.31664 - \frac{0.46568}{Tr}\right) (V_r - 0.5)\right)
$$

$$
\psi = [1 + (\omega - 0.011)(0.39490(V_r - 1.02355) - 0.93281(V_r - 0.75464)\ln T_r]\frac{0.288}{Z_c}
$$

$$
\lambda_1 = 1944 \eta_0
$$

$$
\lambda_2 = \left\{b_1 + b_2\left[b_3 - \ln \left(\frac{T_0}{b_4} \right)\right]^2\right\}\rho_0
$$

$$
\lambda_3 = \exp\left(a_1 + \frac{a_2}{T_0}\right)\left\{\exp[(a_3 + \frac{a_4}{T_0^{1.5}})\rho_0^{0.1} + (\frac{\rho_0}{0.1617} - 1) \rho_0^{0.5}(a_5 + \frac{a_6}{T_0} + \frac{a_7}{T_0^2})] - 1\right\}
$$

$$
\lambda^{**} = [\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3]H
$$

$$
H = \left(\frac{16.04}{MW}\right)^{0.5}f^{0.5}/h^{2/3}
$$

$$
X = \left\{\left[1 - \frac{T}{f}\left(\frac{df}{dT}\right)_v \right] \frac{0.288}{Z_c}\right\}^{1.5}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{df}{dT}\right)_v = \frac{T_c}{190.4}\left(\frac{d\theta} {d T}\right)_v
$$

$$
\left(\frac{d\theta}{d T}\right)_v = (\omega-0.011)\left[ \frac{-0.86276}{T} + (V_r-0.5)\frac{0.46568T_c}{T^2}\right]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the gas [K]

Vc

: Critical volume of the gas [m^3/mol]

Zc

: Critical compressibility of the gas [-]

omega

: Acentric factor of the gas [-]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

Vm

: Volume of the gas at T and P [m^3/mol]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated dense gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

Reference fluid is Methane.
MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Eli_Hanley_dense(T=473., MW=42.081, Tc=364.9, Vc=1.81E-4, Zc=0.274,
... omega=0.144, Cvm=82.70, Vm=1.721E-4)
0.06038475754109959
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Ely, James F., and H. J. M. Hanley. "Prediction of Transport
    Properties. 2. Thermal Conductivity of Pure Fluids and Mixtures."
    Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 22, no. 1 (February 1,
    1983): 90-97. doi:10.1021/i100009a016.

[^footnote-2]: Reid, Robert C.; Prausnitz, John M.; Poling, Bruce E.
    Properties of Gases and Liquids. McGraw-Hill Companies, 1987.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Chung_dense
documentation:
Estimates the thermal conductivity of a gas at high pressure as a
function of temperature using the reference fluid method of
Chung [^footnote-1] as shown in [^footnote-2].

$$
\lambda = \frac{31.2 \eta^\circ \Psi}{M'}(G_2^{-1} + B_6 y)+qB_7y^2T_r^{1/2}G_2
$$

$$
\Psi = 1 + \alpha \left\{[0.215+0.28288\alpha-1.061\beta+0.26665Z]/ [0.6366+\beta Z + 1.061 \alpha \beta]\right\}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{C_v}{R}-1.5
$$

$$
\beta = 0.7862-0.7109\omega + 1.3168\omega^2
$$

$$
Z=2+10.5T_r^2
$$

$$
q = 3.586\times 10^{-3} (T_c/M')^{1/2}/V_c^{2/3}
$$

$$
y = \frac{V_c}{6V}
$$

$$
G_1 = \frac{1-0.5y}{(1-y)^3}
$$

$$
G_2 = \frac{(B_1/y)[1-\exp(-B_4y)]+ B_2G_1\exp(B_5y) + B_3G_1} {B_1B_4 + B_2 + B_3}
$$

$$
B_i = a_i + b_i \omega + c_i \mu_r^4 + d_i \kappa
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the gas [K]

MW

: Molecular weight of the gas [g/mol]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the gas [K]

Vc

: Critical volume of the gas [m^3/mol]

omega

: Acentric factor of the gas [-]

Cvm

: Molar contant volume heat capacity of the gas [J/mol/K]

Vm

: Molar volume of the gas at T and P [m^3/mol]

mu

: Low-pressure gas viscosity [Pa\*s]

dipole

: Dipole moment [debye]

association

: Association factor [-]

# Returns

kg

: Estimated dense gas thermal conductivity [W/m/k]

# Notes

MW internally converted to kg/g-mol.
Vm internally converted to mL/mol.
[^footnote-1] is not the latest form as presented in [^footnote-1].
Association factor is assumed 0. Relates to the polarity of the gas.

Coefficients as follows:

ais = [2.4166E+0, -5.0924E-1, 6.6107E+0, 1.4543E+1, 7.9274E-1, -5.8634E+0, 9.1089E+1]

bis = [7.4824E-1, -1.5094E+0, 5.6207E+0, -8.9139E+0, 8.2019E-1, 1.2801E+1, 1.2811E+2]

cis = [-9.1858E-1, -4.9991E+1, 6.4760E+1, -5.6379E+0, -6.9369E-1, 9.5893E+0, -5.4217E+1]

dis = [1.2172E+2, 6.9983E+1, 2.7039E+1, 7.4344E+1, 6.3173E+0, 6.5529E+1, 5.2381E+2]

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Chung_dense(T=473., MW=42.081, Tc=364.9, Vc=184.6E-6, omega=0.142,
... Cvm=82.67, Vm=172.1E-6, mu=134E-7, dipole=0.4)
0.06160569232570781
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Chung, Ting Horng, Mohammad Ajlan, Lloyd L. Lee, and Kenneth E.
    Starling. "Generalized Multiparameter Correlation for Nonpolar and Polar
    Fluid Transport Properties." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research
    27, no. 4 (April 1, 1988): 671-79. doi:10.1021/ie00076a024.

[^footnote-2]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Lindsay_Bromley
documentation:
Calculates thermal conductivity of a gas mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1] and also in [^footnote-2]. It is significantly more complicated
than other kinetic theory models.

$$
k = \sum_i \frac{y_i k_i}{\sum_j y_i A_{ij}}
$$

$$
A_{ij} = \frac{1}{4} \left\{ 1 + \left[\frac{\eta_i}{\eta_j} \left(\frac{MW_j}{MW_i}\right)^{0.75} \left( \frac{T+S_i}{T+S_j}\right) \right]^{0.5} \right\}^2 \left( \frac{T+S_{ij}}{T+S_i}\right)
$$

$$
S_{ij} = S_{ji} = (S_i S_j)^{0.5}
$$

$$
S_i = 1.5 T_b
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of gas [K]

ys

: Mole fractions of gas components

ks

: Gas thermal conductivites of all components, [W/m/K]

mus

: Gas viscosities of all components, [Pa\*s]

Tbs

: Boiling points of all components, [K]

MWs

: Molecular weights of all components, [g/mol]

# Returns

kg

: Thermal conductivity of gas mixture, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.
The example is from [^footnote-2]; all results agree.
The original source has not been reviewed.

DIPPR Procedure 9D: Method for the Thermal Conductivity of Gas Mixtures

Average deviations of 4-5% for 77 binary mixtures reviewed in [^footnote-2], from
1342 points; also six ternary mixtures (70 points); max deviation observed
was 40%. (DIPPR)

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Lindsay_Bromley(323.15, [0.23, 0.77], [1.939E-2, 1.231E-2], [1.002E-5, 1.015E-5], [248.31, 248.93], [46.07, 50.49])
0.013902644179693132
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Lindsay, Alexander L., and LeRoy A. Bromley. "Thermal Conductivity
    of Gas Mixtures." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry 42, no. 8
    (August 1, 1950): 1508-11. doi:10.1021/ie50488a017.

[^footnote-2]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.

[^footnote-3]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.
## Thermal conductivity of gas mixtures

functions.name: chemicals.thermal_conductivity.Wassiljewa_Herning_Zipperer
documentation:
Calculates thermal conductivity of a gas mixture according to
the kinetic theory expression of Wassiljewa with the interaction
term from the Herning-Zipperer expression. This is also used for
the prediction of gas mixture viscosity.

$$
k = \sum \frac{y_i k_i}{\sum y_i A_{ij}}
$$

$$
A_{ij} = \left(\frac{MW_j}{MW_i}\right)^{0.5}
$$

# Parameters

zs

: Mole fractions of gas components, [-]

ks

: gas thermal conductivites of all components, [W/m/K]

MWs

: Molecular weights of all components, [g/mol]

MW_roots

: Square roots of molecular weights of all components;
  speeds up the calculation if provided, [g^0.5/mol^0.5]

# Returns

kg

: Thermal conductivity of gas mixture, [W/m/K]

# Notes

This equation is entirely dimensionless; all dimensions cancel.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Wassiljewa_Herning_Zipperer(zs=[.1, .4, .5], ks=[1.002E-5, 1.15E-5, 2e-5], MWs=[40.0, 50.0, 60.0])
1.5861181979916883e-05
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.
cmps = range(len(ys))
Ss = [1.5*Tb for Tb in Tbs]
Sij = [[(Si*Sj)**0.5 for Sj in Ss] for Si in Ss]
for j in cmps] for i in cmps]
return sum([ys[i]*ks[i]/sum(ys[j]*Aij[i][j] for j in cmps) for i in cmps])

This module contains various surface tension estimation routines, dataframes
of fit coefficients, fitting model equations, mixing rules, and
water-hydrocarbon interfacial tension estimation routines.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals/).

functions.name: chemicals.interface.sigma_IAPWS
documentation:
Calculate the surface tension of pure water as a function of .
temperature. Assumes the 2011 IAPWS [^footnote-1] formulation.

$$
\sigma = B\tau^\mu(1+b\tau)\\
$$

$$
\tau = 1-T/T_c\\
$$

$$
B = 0.2358 \text{N/m}\\
$$

$$
b = -0.625\\
$$

$$
\mu = 1.256
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of liquid [K]

# Returns

sigma

: Air-liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

This function is valid from the triple temperature to the critical
temperature. No effects for pressure are included in the formulation.
Test values are from IAPWS 2010 book.

The equation is valid from the triple point to the critical point,
647.096 K; but [^footnote-1] also recommends its use down to -25°C.

If a value larger than the critical temperature is input, 0.0 is returned.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> sigma_IAPWS(300.)
0.0716859625271
>>> sigma_IAPWS(450.)
0.0428914991565
>>> sigma_IAPWS(600.)
0.0083756108728
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: IAPWS. 2014. Revised Release on Surface Tension of Ordinary Water
    Substance

functions.name: chemicals.interface.REFPROP_sigma
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the REFPROP_sigma [^footnote-1]
regression-based method. Relatively recent, and most accurate.

$$
\sigma(T)=\sigma_0\left(1-\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^{n_0}+ \sigma_1\left(1-\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^{n_1}+ \sigma_2\left(1-\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^{n_2}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

sigma0

: First emperical coefficient of a fluid

n0

: First emperical exponent of a fluid

sigma1

: Second emperical coefficient of a fluid.

n1

: Second emperical exponent of a fluid.

sigma1

: Third emperical coefficient of a fluid.

n2

: Third emperical exponent of a fluid.

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

Function as implemented in [^footnote-1]. No example necessary; results match
literature values perfectly.
Form of function returns imaginary results when T > Tc; 0 is returned
if this is the case.

When fitting parameters to this function, it is easy to end up with a
fit that returns negative surface tension near but not quite at the
critical point.

# Examples

Parameters for water at 298.15 K

```pycon
>>> REFPROP_sigma(298.15, 647.096, -0.1306, 2.471, 0.2151, 1.233)
0.07205503890847453
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Diky, Vladimir, Robert D. Chirico, Chris D. Muzny, Andrei F.
    Kazakov, Kenneth Kroenlein, Joseph W. Magee, Ilmutdin Abdulagatov, and
    Michael Frenkel. "ThermoData Engine (TDE): Software Implementation of
    the Dynamic Data Evaluation Concept." Journal of Chemical Information
    and Modeling 53, no. 12 (2013): 3418-30. doi:10.1021/ci4005699.
## Regressed coefficient-based functions

functions.name: chemicals.interface.PPDS14
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the [^footnote-1]
emperical (parameter-regressed) method, called the PPDS 14 equation for
surface tension.

$$
\sigma = a_0 \tau^{a_1}(1 + a_2 \tau)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

a0

: Regression parameter, [N/m]

a1

: Regression parameter, [-]

a2

: Regression parameter, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

If this model is fit with `a0` and `a2` as positive values, it is
guaranteed to predict only positive values of `sigma` right up to the
critical point. However, `a2` is often fit to be a negative value.

# Examples

Benzene at 280 K from [^footnote-1]

```pycon
>>> PPDS14(T=280, Tc=562.05, a0=0.0786269, a1=1.28646, a2=-0.112304)
0.030559764256249854
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "ThermoData Engine (TDE103b V10.1) User\`s Guide."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/Help/TDE103b/Eqns-Pure-SurfaceTension/PPDS14.htm>.

[^footnote-2]: Frenkel, Michael, Robert D. Chirico, Vladimir Diky, Xinjian Yan,
    Qian Dong, and Chris Muzny. "ThermoData Engine (TDE): Software
    Implementation of the Dynamic Data Evaluation Concept." Journal of
    Chemical Information and Modeling 45, no. 4 (July 1, 2005): 816-38.
    <https://doi.org/10.1021/ci050067b>.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Watson_sigma
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the Watson [^footnote-1]
emperical (parameter-regressed) method developed by NIST.

$$
\sigma = \exp\left[a_{1} + \ln(1 - T_r)\left( a_2 + a_3T_r + a_4T_r^2 + a_5T_r^3 \right)\right]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

a1

: Regression parameter, [-]

a2

: Regression parameter, [-]

a3

: Regression parameter, [-]

a4

: Regression parameter, [-]

a5

: Regression parameter, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

This expression is also used for enthalpy of vaporization in [^footnote-1].
The coefficients from NIST TDE for enthalpy of vaporization are kJ/mol.

This model is coded to return 0 values at Tr >= 1. It is otherwise not
possible to evaluate this expression at Tr = 1, as log(0) is undefined
(although the limit shows the expression converges to 0).

This equation does not have any term forcing it to become near-zero
at the critical point, but it cannot be fit so as to produce negative
values.

# Examples

Isooctane at 350 K from [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> Watson_sigma(T=350.0, Tc=543.836, a1=-3.02417, a2=1.21792, a3=-5.26877e-9, a4=5.62659e-9, a5=-2.27553e-9)
0.0138340926605649
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "ThermoData Engine (TDE103b V10.1) User\`s Guide."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/Help/TDE103b/Eqns-Pure-SurfaceTension/HVPExpansion-SurfaceTension.htm>

functions.name: chemicals.interface.ISTExpansion
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the IST expansion [^footnote-1]
emperical (parameter-regressed) method developed by NIST.

$$
\sigma = \sum_i a_i\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_c} \right)^i
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

a1

: Regression parameter, [-]

a2

: Regression parameter, [-]

a3

: Regression parameter, [-]

a4

: Regression parameter, [-]

a5

: Regression parameter, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

This equation hsa a term term forcing it to become zero
at the critical point, but it can easily be fit so as to produce negative
values at any reduced temperature.

# Examples

Diethyl phthalate at 400 K from [^footnote-1]:

```pycon
>>> ISTExpansion(T=400.0, Tc=776.0, a1=0.037545, a2=0.0363288)
0.02672100905515996
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: "ThermoData Engine (TDE103b V10.1) User\`s Guide."
    <https://trc.nist.gov/TDE/Help/TDE103b/Eqns-Pure-SurfaceTension/ISTExpansion-SurfaceTension.htm>

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Somayajulu
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the [^footnote-1]
emperical (parameter-regressed) method. Well regressed, no recent data.

$$
\sigma=aX^{5/4}+bX^{9/4}+cX^{13/4}
$$

$$
X=(T_c-T)/T_c
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

A

: Regression parameter

B

: Regression parameter

C

: Regression parameter

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

Presently untested, but matches expected values. Internal units are mN/m.
Form of function returns imaginary results when T > Tc; 0.0 is returned
if this is the case. Function is claimed valid from the triple to the
critical point. Results can be evaluated beneath the triple point.

This function can be accidentally fit to return negative
values of surface tension.

# Examples

Water at 300 K

```pycon
>>> Somayajulu(300, 647.126, 232.713514, -140.18645, -4.890098)
0.07166386387996758
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Somayajulu, G. R. "A Generalized Equation for Surface Tension from
    the Triple Point to the Critical Point." International Journal of
    Thermophysics 9, no. 4 (July 1988): 559-66. doi:10.1007/BF00503154.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Jasper
documentation:
Calculates surface tension of a fluid given two parameters, a linear
fit in Celcius from [^footnote-1] with data reprinted in [^footnote-2].

$$
\sigma = a - bT
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid, [K]

a

: Parameter for equation. Chemical specific.

b

: Parameter for equation. Chemical specific.

# Returns

sigma

: Surface tension [N/m]

# Notes

Internal units are mN/m, and degrees Celcius.
This function has been checked against several references.

As this is a linear model, negative values of surface tension will
eventually arise. 0 is returned in these cases.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Jasper(298.15, 24, 0.0773)
0.0220675
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Jasper, Joseph J. "The Surface Tension of Pure Liquid Compounds."
    Journal of Physical and Chemical Reference Data 1, no. 4
    (October 1, 1972): 841-1010. doi:10.1063/1.3253106.

[^footnote-2]: Speight, James. Lange's Handbook of Chemistry. 16 edition.
    McGraw-Hill Professional, 2005.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Brock_Bird
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the [^footnote-1]
emperical method. Old and tested.

$$
\sigma = P_c^{2/3}T_c^{1/3}Q(1-T_r)^{11/9}
$$

$$
Q = 0.1196 \left[ 1 + \frac{T_{br}\ln (P_c/1.01325)}{1-T_{br}}\right]-0.279
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

Numerous arrangements of this equation are available.
This is DIPPR Procedure 7A: Method for the Surface Tension of Pure,
Nonpolar, Nonhydrocarbon Liquids
The exact equation is not in the original paper.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

p-dichloribenzene at 412.15 K, from DIPPR; value differs due to a slight
difference in method.

```pycon
>>> Brock_Bird(412.15, 447.3, 685, 3.952E6)
0.02208448325192495
```

Chlorobenzene from Poling, as compared with a % error value at 293 K.

```pycon
>>> Brock_Bird(293.15, 404.75, 633.0, 4530000.0)
0.032985686413713036
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Brock, James R., and R. Byron Bird. "Surface Tension and the
    Principle of Corresponding States." AIChE Journal 1, no. 2
    (June 1, 1955): 174-77. doi:10.1002/aic.690010208
## CSP methods

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Pitzer_sigma
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the correlation derived
by [^footnote-1] from the works of [^footnote-2] and [^footnote-3]. Based on critical property CSP
methods.

$$
\sigma = P_c^{2/3}T_c^{1/3}\frac{1.86 + 1.18\omega}{19.05} \left[ \frac{3.75 + 0.91 \omega}{0.291 - 0.08 \omega}\right]^{2/3} (1-T_r)^{11/9}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

The source of this equation has not been reviewed.
Internal units of presure are bar, surface tension of mN/m.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Chlorobenzene from Poling, as compared with a % error value at 293 K.

```pycon
>>> Pitzer_sigma(293., 633.0, 4530000.0, 0.249)
0.03458453513446388
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Poling, Bruce E. The Properties of Gases and Liquids. 5th edition.
    New York: McGraw-Hill Professional, 2000.

[^footnote-2]: Curl, R. F., and Kenneth Pitzer. "Volumetric and Thermodynamic
    Properties of Fluids-Enthalpy, Free Energy, and Entropy." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry 50, no. 2 (February 1, 1958): 265-74.
    doi:10.1021/ie50578a047

[^footnote-3]: Pitzer, K. S.: Thermodynamics, 3d ed., New York, McGraw-Hill,
    1995, p. 521.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Sastri_Rao
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the correlation derived by
[^footnote-1] based on critical property CSP methods and chemical classes.

$$
\sigma = K P_c^xT_b^y T_c^z\left[\frac{1-T_r}{1-T_{br}}\right]^m
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

The source of this equation has not been reviewed.
Internal units of presure are bar, surface tension of mN/m.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Chlorobenzene from Poling, as compared with a % error value at 293 K.

```pycon
>>> Sastri_Rao(293.15, 404.75, 633.0, 4530000.0)
0.03234567739694441
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Sastri, S. R. S., and K. K. Rao. "A Simple Method to Predict
    Surface Tension of Organic Liquids." The Chemical Engineering Journal
    and the Biochemical Engineering Journal 59, no. 2 (October 1995): 181-86.
    doi:10.1016/0923-0467(94)02946-6.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Zuo_Stenby
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the reference fluids
methods of [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma^{(1)} = 40.520(1-T_r)^{1.287}
$$

$$
\sigma^{(2)} = 52.095(1-T_r)^{1.21548}
$$

$$
\sigma_r = \sigma_r^{(1)}+ \frac{\omega - \omega^{(1)}} {\omega^{(2)}-\omega^{(1)}} (\sigma_r^{(2)}-\sigma_r^{(1)})
$$

$$
\sigma = T_c^{1/3}P_c^{2/3}[\exp{(\sigma_r)} -1]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

Presently untested. Have not personally checked the sources.
The reference values for methane and n-octane are from the DIPPR database.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Chlorobenzene

```pycon
>>> Zuo_Stenby(293., 633.0, 4530000.0, 0.249)
0.03345569011871088
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Zuo, You-Xiang, and Erling H. Stenby. "Corresponding-States and
    Parachor Models for the Calculation of Interfacial Tensions." The
    Canadian Journal of Chemical Engineering 75, no. 6 (December 1, 1997):
    1130-37. doi:10.1002/cjce.5450750617

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Hakim_Steinberg_Stiel
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the reference fluids methods
of [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma = 4.60104\times 10^{-7} P_c^{2/3}T_c^{1/3}Q_p \left(\frac{1-T_r}{0.4}\right)^m
$$

$$
Q_p = 0.1574+0.359\omega-1.769\chi-13.69\chi^2-0.51\omega^2+1.298\omega\chi
$$

$$
m = 1.21+0.5385\omega-14.61\chi-32.07\chi^2-1.65\omega^2+22.03\omega\chi
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

StielPolar

: Stiel Polar Factor, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

Original equation for m and Q are used. Internal units are atm and mN/m.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

1-butanol, as compared to value in CRC Handbook of 0.02493.

```pycon
>>> Hakim_Steinberg_Stiel(298.15, 563.0, 4414000.0, 0.59, StielPolar=-0.07872)
0.02190790257519
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Hakim, D. I., David Steinberg, and L. I. Stiel. "Generalized
    Relationship for the Surface Tension of Polar Fluids." Industrial &
    Engineering Chemistry Fundamentals 10, no. 1 (February 1, 1971): 174-75.
    doi:10.1021/i160037a032.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Miqueu
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the methods of [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma = k T_c \left( \frac{N_a}{V_c}\right)^{2/3} (4.35 + 4.14 \omega)t^{1.26}(1+0.19t^{0.5} - 0.487t)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Vc

: Critical volume of fluid [m^3/mol]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

Uses Avogadro's constant and the Boltsman constant.
Internal units of volume are mL/mol and mN/m. However, either a typo
is in the article or author's work, or my value of k is off by 10; this is
corrected nonetheless.
Created with 31 normal fluids, none polar or hydrogen bonded. Has an
AARD of 3.5%.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Bromotrifluoromethane, 2.45 mN/m

```pycon
>>> Miqueu(300., 340.1, 0.000199, 0.1687)
0.003474100774091376
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Miqueu, C, D Broseta, J Satherley, B Mendiboure, J Lachaise, and
    A Graciaa. "An Extended Scaled Equation for the Temperature Dependence
    of the Surface Tension of Pure Compounds Inferred from an Analysis of
    Experimental Data." Fluid Phase Equilibria 172, no. 2 (July 5, 2000):
    169-82. doi:10.1016/S0378-3812(00)00384-8.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Aleem
documentation:
Calculates vapor-liquid surface tension using the correlation derived by
[^footnote-1] based on critical property CSP methods.

$$
\sigma = \phi \frac{MW^{1/3}} {6N_A^{1/3}}\rho_l^{2/3}\left[H_{vap} + C_{p,l}(T_b-T)\right]
$$

$$
\phi = 1 - 0.0047MW + 6.8\times 10^{-6} MW^2
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight [g/mol]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

rhol

: Liquid density at T and P [kg/m^3]

Hvap_Tb

: Mass enthalpy of vaporization at the normal boiling point [kg/m^3]

Cpl

: Liquid heat capacity of the chemical at T [J/kg/K]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid-vapor surface tension [N/m]

# Notes

Internal units of molecuar weight are kg/mol. This model is dimensionally
consistent.

This model does not use the critical temperature. After it predicts a
surface tension of 0 at a sufficiently high temperature, it returns
negative results. The temperature at which this occurs (the "predicted"
critical temperature) can be calculated as follows:

$$
\sigma = 0 \to T_{c,predicted} \text{ at } T_b + \frac{H_{vap}}{Cp_l}
$$

To handle this case, if Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

Because of its dependence on density, it has the potential to model the
effect of pressure on surface tension.

Claims AAD of 4.3%. Developed for normal alkanes. Total of 472 data points.
Behaves worse for higher alkanes. Behaves very poorly overall.

# Examples

Methane at 90 K

```pycon
>>> Aleem(T=90, MW=16.04246, Tb=111.6, rhol=458.7, Hvap_Tb=510870.,
... Cpl=2465.)
0.01669970230131523
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Aleem, W., N. Mellon, S. Sufian, M. I. A. Mutalib, and D. Subbarao.
    "A Model for the Estimation of Surface Tension of Pure Hydrocarbon
    Liquids." Petroleum Science and Technology 33, no. 23-24 (December 17,
    2015): 1908-15. doi:10.1080/10916466.2015.1110593.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Mersmann_Kind_sigma
documentation:
Estimates the surface tension of organic liquid substances
according to the method of [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma^* = \frac{\sigma n_{ass}^{1/3}} {(kT_c)^{1/3} T_{rm}P_c^{2/3}}
$$

$$
\sigma^* = \left(\frac{T_b - T_m}{T_m}\right)^{1/3} \left[6.25(1-T_r) + 31.3(1-T_r)^{4/3}\right]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of the fluid [K]

Tm

: Melting temperature [K]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of the fluid [Pa]

n_associated

: Number of associated molecules in a cluster (2 for alcohols, 1
  otherwise), [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid-vapor surface tension [N/m]

# Notes

In the equation, all quantities must be in SI units. `k` is the boltzman
constant.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

MTBE at STP (the actual value is 0.0181):

```pycon
>>> Mersmann_Kind_sigma(298.15, 164.15, 328.25, 497.1, 3430000.0)
0.016744311449290426
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Mersmann, Alfons, and Matthias Kind. "Prediction of Mechanical and
    Thermal Properties of Pure Liquids, of Critical Data, and of Vapor
    Pressure." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, January 31,
    2017\. <https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.iecr.6b04323>.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.sigma_Gharagheizi_1
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the
equation 4 derived in [^footnote-1] by gene expression programming.

$$
\sigma = 8.948226\times 10^{-4}\left[\frac{A^2}{MW}\sqrt{\frac{A\omega}{MW}} \right]^{0.5}
$$

$$
A = (T_{c} - T - \omega)
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

MW

: Molecular weight [g/mol]

omega

: Acentric factor for fluid, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

This equation may fail before the critical point. In this case it returns 0.0
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Methane at 93 K, point from [^footnote-1]'s supporting material:

```pycon
>>> sigma_Gharagheizi_1(T=95, Tc=190.564, MW=16.04, omega=0.012)
0.0110389739
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gharagheizi, Farhad, Ali Eslamimanesh, Mehdi Sattari, Amir H. Mohammadi,
    and Dominique Richon. "Development of Corresponding States Model for
    Estimation of the Surface Tension of Chemical Compounds." AIChE Journal 59,
    no. 2 (2013): 613-21. <https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.13824>.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.sigma_Gharagheizi_2
documentation:
Calculates air-liquid surface tension using the
equation 6 derived in [^footnote-1] by gene expression programming.

$$
\frac{\sigma}{\text{N}/\text{m}} = 10^{-4}\left(\frac{P_c}{\text{bar}}\right)^{2/3} \left(\frac{T_c}{\text{K}}\right)^{1/3}(1-T_r)^{11/9} \left[7.728729T_{br} + 2.476318\left(T_{br}^3 + \frac{V_{c}}{\text{m}^3 /\text{kmol}}\right) \right]
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

Tb

: Boiling temperature of the fluid [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of fluid [K]

Pc

: Critical pressure of fluid [Pa]

MW

: Molecular weight [g/mol]

Vc

: Critical volume of fluid [m^3/mol]

# Returns

sigma

: Liquid surface tension, N/m

# Notes

This expression gives does converge to 0 at the critical point.
If Tc is larger than T, 0 is returned as the model would return complex
numbers.

# Examples

Methane at 93 K, point from [^footnote-1]'s supporting material:

```pycon
>>> sigma_Gharagheizi_2(T=95, Tb=111.66, Tc=190.564, Pc=45.99e5, Vc=0.0986e-3)
0.01674894057
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Gharagheizi, Farhad, Ali Eslamimanesh, Mehdi Sattari, Amir H. Mohammadi,
    and Dominique Richon. "Development of Corresponding States Model for
    Estimation of the Surface Tension of Chemical Compounds." AIChE Journal 59,
    no. 2 (2013): 613-21. <https://doi.org/10.1002/aic.13824>.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.API10A32
documentation:
Calculates the interfacial tension between
a liquid petroleum fraction and air, using the oil's pseudocritical
temperature and Watson K Characterization factor.

$$
\sigma = \frac{673.7\left[\frac{\left(T_c - T\right)}{T_c}\right]^{1.232}}{K_W}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Liquid temperature, [K]

Tc

: Pseudocritical temperature (or critical temperature if using
  the equation with a pure component), [K]

K_W

: Watson characterization factor

# Returns

sigma

: Air-liquid surface tension, [N/m]

# Notes

[^footnote-1] cautions that this should not be applied to coal liquids,
and that it will give higher errors at pressures above 500 psi.
[^footnote-1] claims this has an average error of 10.7%.

This function converges to zero at `Tc`. If Tc is larger than T,
0 is returned as the model would return complex numbers.

# Examples

Sample problem in Comments on Procedure 10A3.2.1 of [^footnote-1];

```pycon
>>> from fluids.core import F2K, R2K
>>> API10A32(T=F2K(60), Tc=R2K(1334), K_W=12.4)
29.577333312096968
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: API Technical Data Book: General Properties & Characterization.
    American Petroleum Institute, 7E, 2005.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Winterfeld_Scriven_Davis
documentation:
Calculates surface tension of a liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1] and also in [^footnote-2].

$$
\sigma_M = \sum_i \sum_j \frac{1}{V_L^{L2}}\left(x_i V_i \right) \left( x_jV_j\right)\sqrt{\sigma_i\cdot \sigma_j}
$$

# Parameters

xs

: Mole fractions of all components, [-]

sigmas

: Surface tensions of all components, [N/m]

rhoms

: Molar densities of all components, [mol/m^3]

# Returns

sigma

: Air-liquid surface tension of mixture, [N/m]

# Notes

DIPPR Procedure 7C: Method for the Surface Tension of Nonaqueous Liquid
Mixtures

Becomes less accurate as liquid-liquid critical solution temperature is
approached. DIPPR Evaluation: 3-4% AARD, from 107 nonaqueous binary
systems, 1284 points. Internally, densities are converted to kmol/m^3. The
Amgat function is used to obtain liquid mixture density in this equation.

Raises a ZeroDivisionError if either molar volume are zero, and a
ValueError if a surface tensions of a pure component is negative.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Winterfeld_Scriven_Davis([0.1606, 0.8394], [0.01547, 0.02877],
... [8610., 15530.])
0.02496738845043982
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Winterfeld, P. H., L. E. Scriven, and H. T. Davis. "An Approximate
    Theory of Interfacial Tensions of Multicomponent Systems: Applications
    to Binary Liquid-Vapor Tensions." AIChE Journal 24, no. 6
    (November 1, 1978): 1010-14. doi:10.1002/aic.690240610.

[^footnote-2]: Danner, Ronald P, and Design Institute for Physical Property Data.
    Manual for Predicting Chemical Process Design Data. New York, N.Y, 1982.
## Surface Tension Mixtures

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Diguilio_Teja
documentation:
Calculates surface tension of a liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1].

$$
\sigma = 1.002855(T^*)^{1.118091} \frac{T}{T_b} \sigma_r
$$

$$
T^* = \frac{(T_c/T)-1}{(T_c/T_b)-1}
$$

$$
\sigma_r = \sum x_i \sigma_i
$$

$$
T_b = \sum x_i T_{b,i}
$$

$$
T_c = \sum x_i T_{c,i}
$$

# Parameters

T

: Temperature of fluid [K]

xs

: Mole fractions of all components

sigmas_Tb

: Surface tensions of all components at the boiling point, [N/m]

Tbs

: Boiling temperatures of all components, [K]

Tcs

: Critical temperatures of all components, [K]

# Returns

sigma

: Air-liquid surface tension of mixture, [N/m]

# Notes

Simple model, however it has 0 citations. Gives similar results to the
`Winterfeld_Scriven_Davis` model.

Raises a ValueError if temperature is greater than the mixture's critical
temperature or if the given temperature is negative, or if the mixture's
boiling temperature is higher than its critical temperature.

[^footnote-1] claims a 4.63 percent average absolute error on 21 binary and 4
ternary non-aqueous systems. [^footnote-1] also considered Van der Waals mixing
rules for `Tc`, but found it provided a higher error of 5.58%

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Diguilio_Teja(T=298.15, xs=[0.1606, 0.8394],
... sigmas_Tb=[0.01424, 0.02530], Tbs=[309.21, 312.95], Tcs=[469.7, 508.0])
0.025716823875045505
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Diguilio, Ralph, and Amyn S. Teja. "Correlation and Prediction of
    the Surface Tensions of Mixtures." The Chemical Engineering Journal 38,
    no. 3 (July 1988): 205-8. doi:10.1016/0300-9467(88)80079-0.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Weinaug_Katz
documentation:
Calculates surface tension of a liquid mixture according to
mixing rules in [^footnote-1] and also in [^footnote-2]. This is based on the
Parachor concept. This is called the Macleod-Sugden model in some places.

$$
\sigma_M = \left[\sum_i P_i\left( \frac{x_i}{V_{m,l}}  - \frac{y_i}{V_{m,g}}\right) \right]^4
$$

# Parameters

parachors

: Parachors of each component, [N^0.25\*m^2.75/mol]

Vml

: Liquid mixture molar volume, [m^3/mol]

Vmg

: Gas mixture molar volume; this can be set to zero at
  low pressures, [m^3/mol]

xs

: Mole fractions of all components in liquid phase, [-]

xs

: Mole fractions of all components in gas phase, [-]

# Returns

sigma

: Air-liquid surface tension of mixture, [N/m]

# Notes

This expression is efficient and does not require pure component
surface tensions. Its accuracy is dubious.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Weinaug_Katz([5.1e-5, 7.2e-5], Vml=0.000125, Vmg=0.02011, xs=[.4, .6], ys=[.6, .4])
0.06547479150776776
```

Neglect the vapor phase density by setting `Vmg` to a high value:

```pycon
>>> Weinaug_Katz([5.1e-5, 7.2e-5], Vml=0.000125, Vmg=1e100, xs=[.4, .6], ys=[.6, .4])
0.06701752894095361
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Weinaug, Charles F., and Donald L. Katz. "Surface Tensions of
    Methane-Propane Mixtures." Industrial & Engineering Chemistry 35,
    no. 2 (February 1, 1943): 239-246. <https://doi.org/10.1021/ie50398a028>.

[^footnote-2]: Pedersen, Karen Schou, Aage Fredenslund, and Per Thomassen.
    Properties of Oils and Natural Gases. Vol. 5. Gulf Pub Co, 1989.

functions.name: chemicals.interface.Meybodi_Daryasafar_Karimi
documentation:
Calculates the interfacial tension between water and a hydrocabon
liquid according to the correlation of [^footnote-1].

$$
\gamma_{hw} = \left(\frac{A_1 + A_2 \Delta \rho + A_3\Delta\rho^2 + A_4\Delta\rho^3} {A_5 + A_6\frac{T^{A_7}}{T_{c,h}} + A_8T^{A_9}} \right)^{A_{10}}
$$

# Parameters

rho_water

: The density of the aqueous phase, [kg/m^3]

rho_oil

: The density of the hydrocarbon phase, [kg/m^3]

T

: Temperature of the fluid, [K]

Tc

: Critical temperature of the hydrocarbon mixture, [K]

# Returns

sigma

: Hydrocarbon-water surface tension [N/m]

# Notes

Internal units of the equation are g/mL and mN/m.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> Meybodi_Daryasafar_Karimi(980, 760, 580, 914)
0.02893598143089256
```

# References

[^footnote-1]: Kalantari Meybodi, Mahdi, Amin Daryasafar, and Masoud Karimi.
    "Determination of Hydrocarbon-Water Interfacial Tension Using a New
    Empirical Correlation." Fluid Phase Equilibria 415 (May 15, 2016):
    42-50. doi:10.1016/j.fluid.2016.01.037.
## Water-hydrocarbon interfacial tensions

This module contains many heat capacity model equations, heat capacity estimation
equations, enthalpy and entropy integrals of those heat capacity equations,
enthalpy/entropy flash initialization routines, and many dataframes of
coefficients.

For reporting bugs, adding feature requests, or submitting pull requests,
please use the [GitHub issue tracker](https://github.com/CalebBell/chemicals/).

```{contents}
:local: true
```
